Local Employment Cross-tabs

master.original <- read_csv("Data/SLEDS/Masters/After analysis/Master-final-after-mca.csv") %>%
  mutate(ps.grad.location = ifelse(ps.grad.location == "PS grad Region", "PS grad region", ps.grad.location))

master.factor.names <- master.original %>%
  select(PersonID, Gender, economic.status, SpecialEdStatus, RaceEthnicity, english.learner, non.english.home, Dem_Desc, edr, pseo.participant, took.ACT, ap.exam, cte.achievement, attended.ps, ps.grad, ps.grad.InstitutionSector, highest.cred.level, ps.grad.location) 

master.states <- read_csv("Data/SLEDS/Masters/States/annual-seven-states.csv") %>%
  filter(grad.year %in% c("grad.year.1", "grad.year.5", "grad.year.10")) %>%
  select(PersonID, grad.year, states) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = grad.year, values_from = states)

master <- master.states %>%
  left_join(master.factor.names, by = "PersonID")

kable(head(master))
PersonID grad.year.5 grad.year.10 grad.year.1 Gender economic.status SpecialEdStatus RaceEthnicity english.learner non.english.home Dem_Desc edr pseo.participant took.ACT ap.exam cte.achievement attended.ps ps.grad ps.grad.InstitutionSector highest.cred.level ps.grad.location
1439556 Not meaningful, not attending ps Meaningful emp region Attending ps M 1 0 Hispanic 0 1 Town/rural mix EDR 8 - Southwest 0 No 0 CTE Concentrator or Completor Yes No Did not grad Did not graduate ps Did not graduate ps

9657752 Not meaningful, not attending ps Not meaningful, not attending ps Not meaningful, not attending ps M 1 1 Hispanic 0 0 Town/rural mix EDR 6W- Upper Minnesota Valley NA No 0 No CTE Yes No Did not grad Did not graduate ps Did not graduate ps
8919833 Meaningful emp MN Meaningful emp region Attending ps F 1 0 White 0 0 Town/rural mix EDR 6W- Upper Minnesota Valley 0 Yes 1 No CTE Yes Y 4 Associate degree PS grad MN
1594136 After 2023 After 2023 Not meaningful, not attending ps M 1 1 Unknown 0 0 Town/rural mix EDR 6W- Upper Minnesota Valley 0 Yes 0 CTE Participant No No Never attended ps Did not graduate ps Did not graduate ps
7207828 After 2023 After 2023 Attending ps F 1 0 Unknown 0 0 Entirely rural EDR 8 - Southwest 1 Yes 0 CTE Concentrator or Completor Yes No Did not grad Did not graduate ps Did not graduate ps 
kable(names(master))

updated.factor.var.names <-  master.factor.names %>%
  select(-PersonID) %>%
  names() %>%
  c("ps.grad.in.same.ruca", "ps.grad.in.same.edr", "ps.grad.in.same.pr", "ps.grad.in.MN")

numeric.var.names <- master.original %>%
  select(ACTCompositeScore, total.cte.courses.taken, MCA.M:MCA.S, avg.unemp.rate, avg.wages.pct.state) %>%
  names()
  
updated.independent.var.names <- c(updated.factor.var.names, numeric.var.names)

cte.cc.var.names <- master.original %>%
  select(ACTE.SPED:Youth.Apprenticeship) %>%
  names()

dep.var.names <- master %>%
  select(PersonID, grad.year.1, grad.year.5, grad.year.10)

updated.master <- master.original %>%
  select(PersonID, all_of(updated.independent.var.names)) %>%
  left_join(dep.var.names, by = "PersonID") %>%
  mutate(grad.year.1 = ifelse(grad.year.1 == "Meaningful emp EDR", "Meaningful emp region", grad.year.1),
         grad.year.5 = ifelse(grad.year.5 == "Meaningful emp EDR", "Meaningful emp region", grad.year.5),
         grad.year.10 = ifelse(grad.year.10 == "Meaningful emp EDR", "Meaningful emp region", grad.year.10)) %>%
  mutate_at(vars(Dem_Desc:ps.grad.in.MN), as.factor) %>%
  mutate_at(vars(grad.year.1:grad.year.10), as.factor) %>%
  mutate(grad.year.1 = fct_relevel(grad.year.1, "Meaningful emp County", "Meaningful emp region", "Meaningful emp MN", "Attending ps", "Not meaningful, not attending ps", "No MN emp record, not attending ps"),
         grad.year.5 = fct_relevel(grad.year.5, "Meaningful emp County", "Meaningful emp region", "Meaningful emp MN", "Attending ps", "Not meaningful, not attending ps", "No MN emp record, not attending ps"),
         grad.year.10 = fct_relevel(grad.year.10, "Meaningful emp County", "Meaningful emp region", "Meaningful emp MN", "Attending ps", "Not meaningful, not attending ps", "No MN emp record, not attending ps"),
         highest.cred.level = ifelse(ps.grad.InstitutionSector == "Did not grad", "Some college", as.character(highest.cred.level)),
         highest.cred.level = ifelse(ps.grad.InstitutionSector == "Never attended ps", "No college", as.character(highest.cred.level)),
         highest.cred.level = as.factor(highest.cred.level),
         Dem_Desc = fct_relevel(Dem_Desc, "Town/rural mix", "Urban/town/rural mix", "Entirely urban")) %>%
  droplevels()

kable(head(updated.master))

There are a number of “states” in which an individual in this dataset are assigned;

  1. Has meaningful employment
    1. In the same county as their high school.
    2. In the same region as their high school, but not county.
    3. In Minnesota, but not in Southwest Minnesota.
  2. Is attending post-secondary.
  3. Has a MN employment record but it’s not meaningful, and not attending post-secondary.
  4. Does not have a MN employment record and is not attending post-secondary.

There are three “times” in which we checked to see if the individual had meaningful employment within these geographies;

  1. one year after graduating high school
  2. five years after graduation, and
  3. ten years after graduation.

Meaningful employment is determined by whether an individual worked 1,000 hours for an employer during time X. In addition, if the individual is working for an employer but not meaningful at time X but worked 1,000 hours for that employer during another year it’s still considered “meaningful”.

Due to time x potentially being passed the date of the latest data (2023) for some individuals, the analysis below will filter out all individuals where time x is after 2023.

From the multiple correspondence analysis and the principle component analysis we determined that the following independent variables will be included in the analysis.

updated.master %>%
  select(-PersonID, -grad.year.1, -grad.year.5, -grad.year.10) %>%
  lapply(class) 
$Gender
[1] "character"

$economic.status
[1] "numeric"

$SpecialEdStatus
[1] "numeric"

$RaceEthnicity
[1] "character"

$english.learner
[1] "numeric"

$non.english.home
[1] "numeric"

$Dem_Desc
[1] "factor"

$edr
[1] "factor"

$pseo.participant
[1] "factor"

$took.ACT
[1] "factor"

$ap.exam
[1] "factor"

$cte.achievement
[1] "factor"

$attended.ps
[1] "factor"

$ps.grad
[1] "factor"

$ps.grad.InstitutionSector
[1] "factor"

$highest.cred.level
[1] "factor"

$ps.grad.location
[1] "factor"

$ps.grad.in.same.ruca
[1] "factor"

$ps.grad.in.same.edr
[1] "factor"

$ps.grad.in.same.pr
[1] "factor"

$ps.grad.in.MN
[1] "factor"

$ACTCompositeScore
[1] "numeric"

$total.cte.courses.taken
[1] "numeric"

$MCA.M
[1] "numeric"

$MCA.R
[1] "numeric"

$MCA.S
[1] "numeric"

$avg.unemp.rate
[1] "numeric"

$avg.wages.pct.state
[1] "numeric"

Methodology

For this analysis we are keeping it simple. For categorical variables I’ll be using the Cramer’s v-square measure of association. The V^2 value is from 0 to 1 with 0 meaning there’s no association and a value closer to 1 indicates a strong association.

Cramér’s V Value Interpretation
0.00–0.10 Negligible association
0.10–0.20 Weak
0.20–0.40 Moderate
0.40–0.60 Relatively strong
0.60–0.80 Strong
0.80–1.00 Very strong (possibly redundant)

For the variables with continuous values I’ll be using the Wilcoxon two paired groups test. P-value will be used to determine whether we can reject the NULL hypothesis at a .01 level. I’ll also include boxplots to see in which direction there might be influence from the variables.

We are going to split up the analysis into two different datasets. The first dataset will combine 3 of the previous states into one.

  • Has meaningful employment anywhere in Southwest Minnesota, which combines the following states;
    • Meaningful emp County
    • Meaningful emp region

The remaining states will stay the same.

  • Has meaningful employment in Minnesota, but not in Southwest Minnesota
  • Is attending a post-secondary institution.
  • Has a MN employment record but it’s not meaningful, and they are not attending post-secondary.
  • Does not have a MN employment record and is not attending post-secondary.

We have quite a few variables remaining, even after MCA analysis. So let’s take a look at how we are going to proceed and group all of this together to see how these relationships work.

  • Demographics
    • Gender
    • economic.status
    • SpecialEdStatus
    • RaceEthnicity
    • englisher.learner
    • non.english.home
  • High school characteristics
    • Dem_Desc
    • edr
    • avg.unemp.rate
    • avg.wage.pct.state
  • High school accomplishments
    • pseo.participant
    • took.ACT
    • ACTCompositeScore
    • ap.exam
    • MCA scores
      • MCA.M
      • MCA.R
      • MCA.S
    • cte.achievement
    • total.cte.courses.taken
  • Post-secondary
    • attended.ps
    • ps.grad
    • ps.grad.InstitutionSector
    • highest.cred.level
    • ps.grad.location


Meaningful employment in Southwest Minnesota

meaningful.emp.sw <- updated.master %>%
  mutate(grad.year.1 = ifelse(grad.year.1 %in% c("Meaningful emp County", "Meaningful emp EDR"), "Meaningful emp region", as.character(grad.year.1)),
         grad.year.1 = as_factor(grad.year.1),
         grad.year.1 = fct_relevel(grad.year.1, "Meaningful emp region", "Meaningful emp MN", "Attending ps", "Not meaningful, not attending ps", "No MN emp record, not attending ps", "After 2023"),
         grad.year.5 = ifelse(grad.year.5 %in% c("Meaningful emp County", "Meaningful emp EDR"), "Meaningful emp region", as.character(grad.year.5)),
         grad.year.5 = as_factor(grad.year.5),
         grad.year.5 = fct_relevel(grad.year.5, "Meaningful emp region", "Meaningful emp MN", "Attending ps", "Not meaningful, not attending ps", "No MN emp record, not attending ps", "After 2023"),
         grad.year.10 = ifelse(grad.year.10 %in% c("Meaningful emp County", "Meaningful emp EDR"), "Meaningful emp region", as.character(grad.year.10)),
         grad.year.10 = as_factor(grad.year.10),
         grad.year.10 = fct_relevel(grad.year.10, "Meaningful emp region", "Meaningful emp MN", "Attending ps", "Not meaningful, not attending ps", "No MN emp record, not attending ps", "After 2023"),
         highest.cred.level = fct_relevel(highest.cred.level, "No college", "Some college", "Less than Associate Degree", "Associate degree", "Bachelor degree or higher"))

write_csv(meaningful.emp.sw, "Data/SLEDS/Masters/After analysis/Master-meaningful-emp-sw.csv")

Let’s first take a look at proportion of states in each of the time.X. The chart below shows that over half of the graduates are attending post-secondary (57%) one year after graduating high school. The further away from high school graduation results in a bit of an increase in the percentage of individuals who have meaningful employment in the region and in Minnesota, while about a third of graduates do not have a MN employment record and are not going to post-secondary.

prop.states.meaningful.emp.sw <- meaningful.emp.sw %>%
  select(grad.year.1, grad.year.5, grad.year.10) %>%
  pivot_longer(names_to = "grad.year", values_to = "states", 1:3) %>%
  group_by(grad.year, states) %>%
  summarize(n = n()) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  filter(states != "After 2023") %>%
  group_by(grad.year) %>%
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(data_id = seq(n()),
         grad.year = fct_relevel(grad.year, "grad.year.1", "grad.year.5", "grad.year.10"))

prop.states.meaningful.emp.sw.plot <- ggplot(prop.states.meaningful.emp.sw, aes(grad.year, pct, fill = states, group = states)) +
  geom_col_interactive(position = "dodge", aes(data_id = data_id, tooltip = paste(grad.year, "\nState of individuals: ", states, "\nNumber of people in states: ", comma(n), "\nPercent of people in state: ", percent(pct, accuracy = .1), sep = ""))) +
   labs(x="", y = "", color="", title = "Percent of people in each state")+
  scale_y_continuous(labels=scales::percent)+
  theme_bar+
  scale_fill_manual(values = brewer.pal(n = 6, "RdYlBu"),
                    guide = guide_legend(ncol = 2)) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom",
        text = element_text(size = 18))


girafe(ggobj = prop.states.meaningful.emp.sw.plot, width_svg = 10, height_svg = 10) %>%
  girafe_options(opts_selection(type = "none"),
                 opts_sizing(rescale = FALSE))


Let’s examine the variables to see if there are any significant differences in the proportion of individuals in these states across the variable categories or values.


Demographics

In the demographics section we will examine the relationship between that paths taken by individuals and the following variables;

  • Demographics
    • Gender
    • economic.status
    • SpecialEdStatus
    • RaceEthnicity
    • englisher.learner
    • non.english.home


Summary of Demographic Variables and Workforce Participation Outcomes

The following demographic variables were examined in relation to workforce participation outcomes at 1, 5, and 10 years after high school graduation using cross-tabulation analysis and Cramer’s V. Variables are ranked by the strength of their association at year 1, as this represents the period of greatest differentiation across all variables.

1. Economic Status (Cramer’s V: 0.285, 0.127, 0.094) The strongest demographic predictor of workforce participation outcomes, particularly at year 1. Economically disadvantaged students enter the workforce earlier but into less stable employment, while non-disadvantaged students pursue postsecondary education at much higher rates. Differences narrow considerably by years 5 and 10.

2. Special Education Status (Cramer’s V: 0.265, 0.149, 0.097) The second strongest demographic predictor, with a notably distinct pattern of earlier workforce entry into less stable employment and much lower postsecondary attendance. A consistent tendency toward meaningful regional rather than Minnesota employment persists across all three time points.

3. English Learner Status (Cramer’s V: 0.214, 0.086, 0.059) A moderate association at year 1 that weakens considerably over time. English learners enter the workforce earlier but into less stable situations, with persistent challenges in achieving meaningful Minnesota employment through year 10.

4. Non-English Home Language (Cramer’s V: 0.178, 0.058, 0.047) Closely mirrors the English learner pattern across all three time points, consistent with the strong overlap between these variables identified during MCA. Slightly weaker associations than English learner status at all time points.

5. Race/Ethnicity (Cramer’s V: 0.108, 0.044, 0.047) A weak but statistically significant association that declines sharply after year 1. Notable patterns include lower postsecondary attendance among Hispanic and AI students at year 1, persistent workforce challenges for AI students, and higher rates of no Minnesota employment record among all minority groups at year 10.

6. Gender (Cramer’s V: 0.097, 0.093, 0.056) The weakest demographic predictor overall, with consistently weak associations across all three time points. Females are more likely to pursue postsecondary education immediately after graduation while males transition more quickly into regional employment, though differences narrow considerably by year 10.


Gender

Overview Cross-tabulation analysis examined the relationship between gender and workforce participation outcomes at 1, 5, and 10 years after high school graduation. While statistically significant at all three time points, Cramer’s V values indicate a weak association throughout.

Year 1 At one year post-graduation, the most notable gender difference is in postsecondary attendance, with females attending at a higher rate (60.9%) than males (52.1%). Males show higher rates of meaningful regional employment (14.4% vs 10.9%) and are more likely to have no Minnesota employment record (12.0% vs 8.6%), suggesting males transition more quickly into the workforce while females are more likely to pursue postsecondary education immediately after graduation. (χ²=449.730, df=4, Cramer’s V=0.097, p<0.001)

Year 5 By five years post-graduation, gender differences in postsecondary attendance persist but narrow, with females still attending at a higher rate (19.8% vs 14.1%). Males continue to show higher rates of meaningful regional employment (26.2% vs 20.9%), while females show slightly higher rates of no Minnesota employment record (22.6% vs 24.4%). (χ²=303.350, df=4, Cramer’s V=0.093, p<0.001)

Year 10 At ten years post-graduation, gender differences narrow considerably, with Cramer’s V declining to 0.056. Males maintain a modest advantage in meaningful regional employment (26.3% vs 23.2%) and meaningful Minnesota employment (24.7% vs 22.4%), while females show higher rates of no Minnesota employment record (37.8% vs 34.7%), potentially reflecting differences in out-of-state migration or labor force participation patterns. (χ²=61.851, df=3, Cramer’s V=0.056, p<0.001)

Summary Gender shows a statistically significant but consistently weak association with workforce participation outcomes across all three time points. The most notable pattern is that females are more likely to pursue postsecondary education immediately after graduation while males transition more quickly into regional employment, though these differences narrow considerably by year 10.


  • One year after graduating
  • Five years after graduating
  • Ten years after graduating
data <- meaningful.emp.sw %>%
  select(grad.year.1, Gender) %>%
  filter(grad.year.1 != "After 2023") %>%
  droplevels()


tab_xtab(var.row = data$grad.year.1, var.col = data$Gender, 
         title = "", 
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/Claude/analysis_local_employment_ct/Demographics/Gender-1.html",
         use.viewer = TRUE)

χ2=61.851 · df=3 · Cramer's V=0.056 · p=0.000


economic.status

Overview Cross-tabulation analysis examined the relationship between economic status (0 = not economically disadvantaged, 1 = economically disadvantaged) and workforce participation outcomes at 1, 5, and 10 years after high school graduation. Economic status shows the strongest association with workforce participation outcomes of any demographic variable examined, particularly at year 1, though the association weakens considerably over time.

Year 1 At one year post-graduation, economic status shows a moderate association with workforce participation outcomes (Cramer’s V=0.285), the strongest relationship observed across all demographic variables. Non-disadvantaged students are far more likely to be attending postsecondary education (67.4% vs 38.2%), while economically disadvantaged students are more likely to be in meaningful regional employment (17.6% vs 9.7%), not meaningfully employed (23.8% vs 11.9%), or have no Minnesota employment record (14.7% vs 7.7%). This suggests that economic disadvantage is strongly associated with earlier workforce entry but also with less stable employment outcomes. (χ²=3873.212, df=4, Cramer’s V=0.285, p<0.001)

Year 5 By five years post-graduation the association weakens substantially (Cramer’s V=0.127). Non-disadvantaged students maintain a higher rate of postsecondary attendance (19.8% vs 11.9%) and meaningful Minnesota employment (19.5% vs 16.0%), while economically disadvantaged students show higher rates of meaningful regional employment (25.3% vs 22.6%) and not meaningful employment (21.6% vs 15.7%). (χ²=565.544, df=4, Cramer’s V=0.127, p<0.001)

Year 10 At ten years post-graduation the association weakens further (Cramer’s V=0.094), approaching the weak associations observed for gender. The most notable remaining difference is in meaningful Minnesota employment, where non-disadvantaged students show a higher rate (25.6% vs 19.6%), and not meaningful employment, where economically disadvantaged students show a higher rate (19.4% vs 13.5%). Rates of meaningful regional employment and no Minnesota employment record converge substantially between the two groups. (χ²=176.145, df=3, Cramer’s V=0.094, p<0.001)

Summary Economic status is the strongest demographic predictor of workforce participation outcomes, particularly at year 1 where it shows a moderate association (Cramer’s V=0.285). The primary early pattern is that economically disadvantaged students enter the workforce sooner but with less stable outcomes, while non-disadvantaged students are more likely to pursue postsecondary education. These differences narrow considerably by years 5 and 10, suggesting that economic disadvantage has its greatest influence on the immediate post-graduation transition rather than long-term workforce participation.

  • One year after graduating
  • Five years after graduating
  • Ten years after graduating
data <- meaningful.emp.sw %>%
  select(grad.year.1, economic.status) %>%
  filter(grad.year.1 != "After 2023") %>%
  droplevels()

tab_xtab(var.row = data$grad.year.1, var.col = data$economic.status, 
         title = "", 
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/Claude/analysis_local_employment_ct/Demographics/economic-status-1.html")

SpecialEdStatus

Overview Cross-tabulation analysis examined the relationship between special education status (0 = non-special education, 1 = special education) and workforce participation outcomes at 1, 5, and 10 years after high school graduation. Special education status shows a moderate association at year 1 that weakens considerably over time, with a notably distinct pattern compared to non-special education students in early post- graduation years.

Year 1 At one year post-graduation, special education status shows a moderate association with workforce participation outcomes (Cramer’s V=0.265). Non-special education students are far more likely to be attending postsecondary education (61.4% vs 25.3%), while special education students show considerably higher rates of not meaningful employment (31.2% vs 14.0%), meaningful regional employment (17.6% vs 11.9%), and no Minnesota employment record (21.5% vs 8.6%). This suggests that special education students transition out of education much more quickly but into less stable employment situations. (χ²=3359.009, df=4, Cramer’s V=0.265, p<0.001)

Year 5 By five years post-graduation the association weakens (Cramer’s V=0.149). Special education students show higher rates of meaningful regional employment (28.1% vs 22.9%) and not meaningful employment (25.6% vs 16.6%), while non-special education students show higher rates of postsecondary attendance (18.6% vs 5.9%) and meaningful Minnesota employment (19.2% vs 11.9%). The higher rate of meaningful regional employment among special education students is a notable finding, suggesting this group gravitates toward local employment when they do find stable work. (χ²=783.272, df=4, Cramer’s V=0.149, p<0.001)

Year 10 At ten years post-graduation the association weakens considerably (Cramer’s V=0.097). Special education students continue to show higher rates of meaningful regional employment (27.4% vs 24.4%) and not meaningful employment (22.9% vs 14.5%), while non-special education students show notably higher rates of meaningful Minnesota employment (24.7% vs 15.5%). Rates of no Minnesota employment record converge substantially between the two groups (34.2% vs 36.5%). (χ²=188.532, df=3, Cramer’s V=0.097, p<0.001)

Summary Special education status shows a moderate association with workforce participation outcomes at year 1 (Cramer’s V=0.265) that weakens over time, approaching weak levels by year 10 (Cramer’s V=0.097). A consistent and notable pattern across all three time points is that special education students show a stronger tendency toward meaningful regional employment relative to meaningful Minnesota employment, suggesting this group is more likely to remain in the local region when they do achieve stable workforce participation. The most striking early difference is the much lower rate of postsecondary attendance among special education students, which has largely resolved by year 5.

  • One year after graduating
  • Five years after graduating
  • Ten years after graduating
data <- meaningful.emp.sw %>%
  select(grad.year.1, SpecialEdStatus) %>%
  filter(grad.year.1 != "After 2023") %>%
  droplevels()


tab_xtab(var.row = data$grad.year.1, var.col = data$SpecialEdStatus, 
         title = "", 
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/Claude/analysis_local_employment_ct/Demographics/SpecialEdStatus-1.html")

RaceEthnicity

Overview Cross-tabulation analysis examined the relationship between race/ethnicity and workforce participation outcomes at 1, 5, and 10 years after high school graduation. While statistically significant at all three time points, Cramer’s V values indicate a weak association overall, with the strength of association declining sharply from year 1 to years 5 and 10. Interpretation of minority group results should be made with caution given the relatively small sample sizes for AI, Asian/PI, Black, and Hispanic students in this Southwest Minnesota dataset.

Year 1 At one year post-graduation, race/ethnicity shows a weak but meaningful association with workforce participation outcomes (Cramer’s V=0.108). White students show the highest rate of postsecondary attendance (60.7%), compared to considerably lower rates for Hispanic (28.0%), Black (44.4%), and AI students (31.7%). Hispanic (20.1%) and Black (18.4%) students show notably higher rates of meaningful regional employment than White students (11.5%), while AI students show the highest combined rates of not meaningful employment (30.6%) and no Minnesota employment record (26.2%), suggesting significant early workforce challenges for this group. (χ²=1942.573, df=16, Cramer’s V=0.108, p<0.001)

Year 5 By five years post-graduation the association weakens dramatically (Cramer’s V=0.044). Hispanic students show the highest rate of meaningful regional employment (25.2%), while AI students show the lowest rate of meaningful Minnesota employment (7.8%) and the highest combined rates of not meaningful employment (27.3%) and no Minnesota employment record (28.6%). Asian/PI and Black students show relatively higher rates of meaningful Minnesota employment (18.1% and 19.1% respectively), suggesting these groups may be pursuing employment outside the region at higher rates. (χ²=278.357, df=16, Cramer’s V=0.044, p<0.001)

Year 10 At ten years post-graduation the association remains weak (Cramer’s V=0.047). A notable pattern emerges with Asian/PI (42.6%), Black (39.1%), Hispanic (40.0%), and AI (37.6%) students all showing higher rates of no Minnesota employment record compared to White students (35.7%), suggesting higher rates of out-of-state migration or labor force non-participation among minority groups in the long term. AI students continue to show the lowest rate of meaningful Minnesota employment (10.1%), while Asian/PI and Black students maintain relatively higher rates of meaningful Minnesota employment (25.0% and 23.0% respectively). (χ²=133.122, df=12, Cramer’s V=0.047, p<0.001)

Summary Race/ethnicity shows a statistically significant but consistently weak association with workforce participation outcomes across all three time points, with the association declining sharply after year 1. The most notable patterns are the lower rates of postsecondary attendance among Hispanic and AI students at year 1, the persistent workforce challenges faced by AI students across all time points, and the higher rates of no Minnesota employment record among all minority groups at year 10 relative to White students. The small sample sizes for minority groups in this Southwest Minnesota dataset warrant caution in interpreting these findings.

  • One year after graduating
  • Five years after graduating
  • Ten years after graduating
data <- meaningful.emp.sw %>%
  select(grad.year.1, RaceEthnicity) %>%
  filter(grad.year.1 != "After 2023",
         RaceEthnicity != "Unknown") %>%
  droplevels()

                 title = "", 
                 show.exp = TRUE,
                 show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/Claude/analysis_local_employment_ct/Demographics/RaceEthnicity-1.html")

χ2=133.122 · df=12 · Cramer's V=0.047 · p=0.000


english.learner

Overview Cross-tabulation analysis examined the relationship between English learner status (0 = non-English learner, 1 = English learner) and workforce participation outcomes at 1, 5, and 10 years after high school graduation. English learner status shows a moderate association at year 1 that weakens considerably over time, with a pattern broadly similar to other disadvantage-related demographic variables.

Year 1 At one year post-graduation, English learner status shows a moderate association with workforce participation outcomes (Cramer’s V=0.214). Non-English learners are far more likely to be attending postsecondary education (59.8% vs 25.5%), while English learners show considerably higher rates of meaningful regional employment (20.5% vs 11.9%), not meaningful employment (25.1% vs 15.4%), and no Minnesota employment record (23.2% vs 9.0%). This pattern is consistent with English learners entering the workforce more quickly after graduation but into less stable employment situations, mirroring patterns observed for economically disadvantaged and special education students. (χ²=2184.081, df=4, Cramer’s V=0.214, p<0.001)

Year 5 By five years post-graduation the association weakens substantially (Cramer’s V=0.086). Rates of meaningful regional employment converge almost entirely between English learners (24.1%) and non-English learners (23.5%). However, English learners continue to show lower rates of postsecondary attendance (8.2% vs 17.6%) and meaningful Minnesota employment (14.2% vs 18.5%), and higher rates of not meaningful employment (23.6% vs 17.3%) and no Minnesota employment record (29.8% vs 23.0%), suggesting persistent challenges in achieving stable employment outside the region. (χ²=259.548, df=4, Cramer’s V=0.086, p<0.001)

Year 10 At ten years post-graduation the association weakens further (Cramer’s V=0.059). The most notable remaining differences are in meaningful Minnesota employment (14.4% vs 24.0%) and no Minnesota employment record (41.9% vs 35.9%), suggesting English learners are less likely to achieve stable employment in Minnesota and more likely to have no Minnesota employment record in the long term. Rates of meaningful regional employment are similar between the two groups (21.9% vs 24.9%). (χ²=69.298, df=3, Cramer’s V=0.059, p<0.001)

Summary English learner status shows a moderate association with workforce participation outcomes at year 1 (Cramer’s V=0.214) that weakens considerably over time. The primary pattern is that English learners enter the workforce more quickly after graduation but into less stable employment situations, with lower rates of postsecondary attendance and higher rates of not meaningful employment and no Minnesota employment record persisting through year 10. A notable finding is that rates of meaningful regional employment converge substantially between English learners and non-English learners by year 5, suggesting that English learners who do achieve stable employment are roughly as likely as non-English learners to find it within the region.

  • One year after graduating
  • Five years after graduating
  • Ten years after graduating
data <- meaningful.emp.sw %>%
  select(grad.year.1, english.learner) %>%
  filter(grad.year.1 != "After 2023") %>%
  droplevels()


tab_xtab(var.row = data$grad.year.1, var.col = data$english.learner, 
         title = "", 
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/Claude/analysis_local_employment_ct/Demographics/english-learner-1.html")

100 %
χ2=2184.081 · df=4 · Cramer's V=0.214 · p=0.000

non.english.home

Overview Cross-tabulation analysis examined the relationship between non-English home language (0 = English speaking home, 1 = non-English speaking home) and workforce participation outcomes at 1, 5, and 10 years after high school graduation. The pattern of results closely mirrors that of English learner status, which is expected given the strong overlap between these two variables identified during MCA. The association is moderate at year 1 and weakens considerably over time.

Year 1 At one year post-graduation, non-English home language shows a moderate association with workforce participation outcomes (Cramer’s V=0.178), somewhat weaker than the English learner association at the same time point. Students from English speaking homes are far more likely to be attending postsecondary education (59.1% vs 31.0%), while students from non-English speaking homes show higher rates of meaningful regional employment (19.6% vs 12.0%), not meaningful employment (21.5% vs 15.8%), and no Minnesota employment record (22.1% vs 9.1%), consistent with earlier workforce entry but less stable employment outcomes. (χ²=1519.246, df=4, Cramer’s V=0.178, p<0.001)

Year 5 By five years post-graduation the association weakens dramatically (Cramer’s V=0.058). Rates of meaningful regional employment converge almost entirely between the two groups (22.6% vs 23.7%). Students from non-English speaking homes continue to show lower rates of postsecondary attendance (12.8% vs 17.3%) and meaningful Minnesota employment (14.5% vs 18.5%), and higher rates of no Minnesota employment record (29.7% vs 22.9%), patterns nearly identical to those observed for English learners at year 5. (χ²=120.330, df=4, Cramer’s V=0.058, p<0.001)

Year 10 At ten years post-graduation the association is very weak (Cramer’s V=0.047). The most notable remaining differences are in meaningful Minnesota employment (18.4% vs 23.9%) and no Minnesota employment record (42.5% vs 35.7%), consistent with the patterns observed for English learner status at year 10. Rates of meaningful regional employment remain similar between the two groups (21.5% vs 25.0%). (χ²=45.168, df=3, Cramer’s V=0.047, p<0.001)

Summary Non-English home language shows a moderate association with workforce participation outcomes at year 1 (Cramer’s V=0.178) that weakens considerably over time, reaching very weak levels by year 10 (Cramer’s V=0.047). The results closely mirror those observed for English learner status across all three time points, consistent with the strong overlap between these variables identified during MCA. The primary persistent pattern is a lower rate of meaningful Minnesota employment and higher rate of no Minnesota employment record among students from non-English speaking homes, while rates of meaningful regional employment converge substantially by year 5, suggesting that when students from non-English speaking homes do achieve stable employment they are roughly as likely as their peers to find it within the region.

  • One year after graduating
  • Five years after graduating
  • Ten years after graduating

High school characteristics

We will take a look at the following variables;

  • High school characteristics
    • Dem_Desc
    • edr
    • avg.unemp.rate
    • avg.wage.pct.state


Summary of High School Characteristics Variables and Workforce Participation Outcomes

The following high school characteristics variables were examined in relation to workforce participation outcomes at 1, 5, and 10 years after high school graduation. Categorical variables were examined using cross-tabulation and Cramer’s V, while continuous variables were examined using one-way ANOVA with eta-squared as the measure of effect size. Variables are presented in two separate ranked lists reflecting their respective effect size measures.

Categorical Variables - Ranked by Cramer’s V at Year 1

  1. EDR (Cramer’s V: 0.079, 0.110, 0.124) The stronger of the two categorical predictors and notable for its increasing association over time. EDR 6E Southwest Central students show a persistent and growing advantage in meaningful Minnesota employment, while EDR 8 Southwest students show increasing rates of no Minnesota employment record over time, suggesting higher out-of-state migration from the more rural southwestern portion of the region.

  2. Community Demographic Description (Cramer’s V: 0.057, 0.072, 0.079) A weak but consistently strengthening association across all three time points. Urban/town/rural mix students increasingly gravitate toward meaningful Minnesota employment outside the region, while town/rural mix and entirely rural students show stronger and more persistent attachment to regional employment.

Continuous Variables - Ranked by Eta-Squared at Year 1

  1. Average Wages as Percentage of State Average (η²: 0.010, 0.004, 0.006) Despite being the stronger of the two continuous predictors at year 1, the effect size is negligible across all time points, explaining at most 1% of variance in workforce participation outcomes. A consistent finding is that meaningful regional employment shows higher relative wages than meaningful Minnesota employment across all three time points, suggesting Southwest Minnesota offers competitive wages relative to the broader state labor market.

  2. Average Unemployment Rate (η²: 0.005, 0.015, 0.032) The weaker predictor at year 1 but showing a growing association over time, reaching a small effect size by year 10. The most consistent finding is that individuals with no Minnesota employment record experience lower average unemployment rates, likely reflecting out-of-state migration to stronger labor markets.

Overall Pattern Both categorical and continuous high school characteristics variables show weak to negligible associations with workforce participation outcomes, with effect sizes that are among the smallest observed across all variable groups examined. The most substantively meaningful finding is the persistent geographic sorting pattern across both EDR and community type — students from more urban and centrally located communities show increasing tendencies toward employment outside the region, while students from more rural and peripheral communities show stronger regional workforce attachment. This geographic sorting strengthens over time and represents the most actionable finding from the high school characteristics group for regional workforce development planning.


Dem_Desc

Overview Cross-tabulation analysis examined the relationship between community demographic description (Town/rural mix, Urban/town/rural mix, Entirely rural) and workforce participation outcomes at 1, 5, and 10 years after high school graduation. Community type shows a weak but statistically significant association across all three time points, with the association actually strengthening slightly from year 1 to years 5 and 10, an unusual pattern relative to the demographic variables examined previously.

Year 1 At one year post-graduation, community type shows a weak association with workforce participation outcomes (Cramer’s V=0.057). The most notable difference is that entirely rural students show a higher rate of postsecondary attendance (64.3%) compared to town/rural mix (55.1%) and urban/town/rural mix (55.5%) students. Town/rural mix students show the highest rate of meaningful regional employment (13.8%), while urban/town/rural mix students show the highest rate of not meaningful employment (18.4%). (χ²=309.223, df=8, Cramer’s V=0.057, p<0.001)

Year 5 By five years post-graduation the association strengthens modestly (Cramer’s V=0.072). Urban/town/rural mix students show a notably higher rate of meaningful Minnesota employment (25.4% vs 17.3% and 15.4%), suggesting students from more urban communities are more likely to find employment outside the region. Town/rural mix students show the highest rate of meaningful regional employment (25.0%), while entirely rural students show the highest rate of no Minnesota employment record (27.4%). (χ²=363.860, df=8, Cramer’s V=0.072, p<0.001)

Year 10 At ten years post-graduation the association remains weak but statistically significant (Cramer’s V=0.079). The most striking pattern is that urban/town/rural mix students show a much higher rate of meaningful Minnesota employment (33.2% vs 22.0% and 20.9%) but a notably lower rate of meaningful regional employment (16.5% vs 26.4% and 25.7%), confirming that students from more urban communities are considerably more likely to find employment outside the region in the long term. Entirely rural students show the highest rate of no Minnesota employment record (38.6%). (χ²=250.931, df=6, Cramer’s V=0.079, p<0.001)

Summary Community type shows a weak but statistically significant and remarkably consistent association with workforce participation outcomes across all three time points, with the strength of association slightly increasing over time — the opposite pattern from most demographic variables. The most substantively important finding is the persistent and growing divergence between urban/town/ rural mix students, who increasingly gravitate toward meaningful Minnesota employment outside the region, and town/rural mix and entirely rural students, who show stronger attachment to regional employment. This geographic sorting pattern becomes more pronounced at years 5 and 10, suggesting community of origin has a lasting influence on where individuals ultimately settle for employment.

  • One year after graduating
  • Five years after graduating
  • Ten years after graduating

edr

Overview Cross-tabulation analysis examined the relationship between Economic Development Region (EDR 6E Southwest Central, EDR 6W Upper Minnesota Valley, EDR 8 Southwest) and workforce participation outcomes at 1, 5, and 10 years after high school graduation. EDR shows a weak association at year 1 that strengthens notably over time, mirroring the pattern observed for community demographic description and suggesting that geographic origin has an increasing influence on long-term workforce participation outcomes.

Year 1 At one year post-graduation, EDR shows a weak association with workforce participation outcomes (Cramer’s V=0.079). EDR 8 Southwest students show the highest rate of postsecondary attendance (60.2%) while EDR 6E Southwest Central students show the lowest (51.6%). EDR 6E students show the highest rates of meaningful regional employment (14.2%) and not meaningful employment (19.4%), while EDR 8 Southwest students show the lowest rate of meaningful Minnesota employment (2.9%) compared to EDR 6E (5.8%) and EDR 6W (3.9%). (χ²=600.491, df=8, Cramer’s V=0.079, p<0.001)

Year 5 By five years post-graduation the association strengthens considerably (Cramer’s V=0.110). The most striking pattern is the divergence in meaningful Minnesota employment, with EDR 6E Southwest Central students showing a much higher rate (25.0%) compared to EDR 6W (15.5%) and EDR 8 Southwest (13.9%). EDR 8 Southwest students show the highest rate of no Minnesota employment record (27.4%), while EDR 6E shows the lowest (17.9%), suggesting that students from the Southwest Central region are considerably more likely to remain employed in Minnesota. (χ²=858.413, df=8, Cramer’s V=0.110, p<0.001)

Year 10 At ten years post-graduation the association strengthens further (Cramer’s V=0.124). EDR 6E Southwest Central students show a notably higher rate of meaningful Minnesota employment (32.4% vs 21.1% and 17.4%), while EDR 8 Southwest students show the highest rate of no Minnesota employment record (42.1% vs 35.7% and 29.3%). Rates of meaningful regional employment are relatively similar across all three EDRs (22.2%, 26.7%, and 26.0%), suggesting that when students from any region do achieve meaningful regional employment they do so at comparable rates, but EDR 6E students are considerably more likely to find stable employment elsewhere in Minnesota rather than leaving the state entirely. (χ²=612.569, df=6, Cramer’s V=0.124, p<0.001)

Summary EDR shows a weak but growing association with workforce participation outcomes across all three time points, with Cramer’s V increasing from 0.079 at year 1 to 0.124 at year 10. The most substantively important finding is the persistent and growing advantage of EDR 6E Southwest Central students in achieving meaningful Minnesota employment, likely reflecting the influence of Marshall and other larger urban centers in that region providing broader employment opportunities. EDR 8 Southwest students show a consistent and growing tendency toward no Minnesota employment record over time, suggesting higher rates of out-of-state migration from the more rural southwestern portion of the region. These geographic patterns complement and reinforce the findings observed for community demographic description.

  • One year after graduating
  • Five years after graduating
  • Ten years after graduating

not attending ps		 2110

100 %
χ2=612.569 · df=6 · Cramer's V=0.124 · p=0.000


avg.unemp.rate

Overview One-way ANOVA examined differences in average unemployment rate experienced by individuals across workforce participation outcome groups at 1, 5, and 10 years after high school graduation. While statistically significant at all three time points, eta-squared values indicate a small and growing association, suggesting that average unemployment rate explains a modest but increasing proportion of variance in workforce participation outcomes over time.

Year 1 At one year post-graduation, average unemployment rate differs significantly across outcome groups (F=58.91, df=4, p<0.001), however the effect size is small (η²=0.005), indicating that unemployment rate explains less than 1% of the variance in workforce participation outcomes at this time point. Tukey HSD post-hoc tests reveal that individuals in the not meaningful employment group experienced significantly higher average unemployment rates than all other groups, while no significant difference was found between the attending postsecondary and no Minnesota employment record groups.

Year 5 By five years post-graduation the association grows modestly (F=128.82, df=4, p<0.001, η²=0.015), remaining a small effect. The meaningful Minnesota employment group is now clearly distinguished from all other groups, experiencing significantly higher average unemployment rates than the meaningful regional employment group. The no Minnesota employment record group experiences significantly lower average unemployment rates than all other groups except meaningful regional employment, suggesting individuals with no Minnesota employment record may have relocated to areas with lower unemployment.

Year 10 At ten years post-graduation the association reaches its strongest point (F=217.93, df=3, p<0.001, η²=0.032), though still representing a small effect by conventional benchmarks. The no Minnesota employment record group continues to experience significantly lower average unemployment rates than all other groups, while the meaningful Minnesota employment group experiences significantly higher unemployment rates than the meaningful regional employment group, suggesting that individuals remaining in Minnesota but outside the region face a more challenging labor market environment over time.

Summary Average unemployment rate shows a statistically significant but small association with workforce participation outcomes across all three time points (η²=0.005, 0.015, 0.032), with the effect size growing over time. The most consistent finding is that individuals with no Minnesota employment record experience lower average unemployment rates, likely reflecting out-of-state migration to areas with stronger labor markets. While the growing divergence between meaningful regional and meaningful Minnesota employment groups over time is noteworthy, the small effect sizes suggest that average unemployment rate alone explains only a modest portion of workforce participation outcomes.

  • One year after graduation
  • Five years after graduating
  • Ten years after graduating
data <- meaningful.emp.sw %>%
  select(grad.year.1, avg.unemp.rate) %>%
  filter(grad.year.1 != "After 2023") %>%
  drop_na() %>%
  group_by(grad.year.1) %>%
  mutate(q1 = quantile(avg.unemp.rate, .25),
         q3 = quantile(avg.unemp.rate, .75),
         iqr = IQR(avg.unemp.rate),
         low.outlier = q1 - 1.5*iqr,
         high.outlier = q3 + 1.5*iqr) %>%
  filter(avg.unemp.rate > low.outlier,
         avg.unemp.rate < high.outlier) %>%
  ungroup()

labels <- data %>%
  group_by(grad.year.1) %>%
  summarize(Min = quantile(avg.unemp.rate, .0),
            Q1 = quantile(avg.unemp.rate, .25),
            Median = median(avg.unemp.rate),
            Q3 = quantile(avg.unemp.rate, .75),
            Max = quantile(avg.unemp.rate, 1),
            Mean = mean(avg.unemp.rate)) %>%
  ungroup()

plot <- ggplot(data, aes(grad.year.1, avg.unemp.rate, fill = grad.year.1, group = grad.year.1)) +
  geom_boxplot_interactive() +
  geom_label(data = labels, aes(x = grad.year.1, y = 15, label = paste("Min: ", comma(Min, accuracy = .1), "\nQ1: ", comma(Q1, accuracy = .1), "\nMedian: ", comma(Median, accuracy = .1), "\nQ3: ", comma(Q3, accuracy = .1), "\nMax: ", comma(Max, accuracy = ), "\nMean: ", comma(Mean, accuracy = .1))), color = "white") +
  labs(x="", y = "", color="", title = "Boxplots of average unemployment by employment state")+
  scale_y_continuous(labels=scales::comma,
                     limits = c(0, 20))+
  scale_fill_manual(values = brewer.pal(n = 6, "RdYlBu")) +
  theme_bar+
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        text = element_text(size = 18),
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 25, hjust = .9))


girafe(ggobj = plot, width_svg = 10, height_svg = 10) %>%
  girafe_options(opts_selection(type = "none"),
                 opts_sizing(rescale = FALSE)) 
summary <- aov(avg.unemp.rate ~ grad.year.1, data = data)

summary
Call:
   aov(formula = avg.unemp.rate ~ grad.year.1, data = data)

Terms:
                grad.year.1 Residuals
Sum of Squares       307.77  60805.42
Deg. of Freedom           4     46551

Residual standard error: 1.142896
Estimated effects may be unbalanced
anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)
  Tukey multiple comparisons of means
    95% family-wise confidence level

Fit: aov(formula = avg.unemp.rate ~ grad.year.1, data = data)

tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/Claude/analysis_local_employment_ct/High school characteristics/avg-unemp-rate-1.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)
data <- meaningful.emp.sw %>%
  select(grad.year.5, avg.unemp.rate) %>%
  filter(grad.year.5 != "After 2023") %>%

  mutate(q1 = quantile(avg.unemp.rate, .25),
         q3 = quantile(avg.unemp.rate, .75),

         low.outlier = q1 - 1.5*iqr,
         high.outlier = q3 + 1.5*iqr) %>%

         avg.unemp.rate < high.outlier) %>%
  ungroup()

            Q1 = quantile(avg.unemp.rate, .25),

            Q3 = quantile(avg.unemp.rate, .75),

  select(grad.year.10, avg.unemp.rate) %>%
  filter(grad.year.10 != "After 2023") %>%
  drop_na() %>%
  group_by(grad.year.10) %>%
  mutate(q1 = quantile(avg.unemp.rate, .25),
         q3 = quantile(avg.unemp.rate, .75),
         iqr = IQR(avg.unemp.rate),
         low.outlier = q1 - 1.5*iqr,
         high.outlier = q3 + 1.5*iqr) %>%
  filter(avg.unemp.rate > low.outlier,
         avg.unemp.rate < high.outlier) %>%
  ungroup()

labels <- data %>%
  group_by(grad.year.10) %>%
  summarize(Min = quantile(avg.unemp.rate, .0),
            Q1 = quantile(avg.unemp.rate, .25),
            Median = median(avg.unemp.rate),
            Q3 = quantile(avg.unemp.rate, .75),
            Max = quantile(avg.unemp.rate, 1),

  geom_boxplot_interactive() +

avg.wages.pct.state

Overview One-way ANOVA examined differences in average wages as a percentage of the state average across workforce participation outcome groups at 1, 5, and 10 years after high school graduation. While statistically significant at all three time points, eta-squared values indicate a very small association throughout, suggesting that average wages as a percentage of state average explains only a negligible proportion of variance in workforce participation outcomes.

Year 1 At one year post-graduation, average wages as a percentage of state average differs significantly across outcome groups (F=115.79, df=4, p<0.001), however the effect size is very small (η²=0.010), indicating that this variable explains approximately 1% of the variance in workforce participation outcomes. Tukey HSD post-hoc tests reveal that the attending postsecondary group shows the highest relative wages, significantly higher than both meaningful regional employment and meaningful Minnesota employment groups. The meaningful regional employment group shows significantly higher relative wages than both the meaningful Minnesota employment and not meaningful employment groups, suggesting that regional employment offers competitive wages relative to other employment outcomes at this early stage.

Year 5 By five years post-graduation the association weakens further (F=38.20, df=4, p<0.001, η²=0.004), explaining less than half a percent of variance in workforce participation outcomes. The meaningful regional employment group continues to show significantly higher relative wages than the meaningful Minnesota employment group, while no significant difference is found between meaningful regional employment and either the attending postsecondary or no Minnesota employment record groups, suggesting wages in the region are converging with state averages for those in stable employment by year 5.

Year 10 At ten years post-graduation the association remains very small (F=41.17, df=3, p<0.001, η²=0.006). The meaningful regional employment group continues to show significantly higher relative wages than the meaningful Minnesota employment group and not meaningful employment group. No significant difference is found between meaningful regional employment and the no Minnesota employment record group, consistent with the pattern observed at year 5.

Summary Average wages as a percentage of state average shows a statistically significant but negligible association with workforce participation outcomes across all three time points (η²=0.010, 0.004, 0.006). Despite the very small effect sizes, a consistent and substantively interesting finding is that the meaningful regional employment group shows significantly higher relative wages than the meaningful Minnesota employment group across all three time points, suggesting that regional employment in Southwest Minnesota offers competitive wages relative to employment elsewhere in the state. However, given the negligible effect sizes, this variable contributes very little independent explanatory power to understanding workforce participation outcomes.

  • One year after graduating
  • Five years after graduating
  • Ten years after graduating
data <- meaningful.emp.sw %>%
  select(grad.year.1, avg.wages.pct.state) %>%
  filter(grad.year.1 != "After 2023") %>%
  drop_na() %>%
  group_by(grad.year.1) %>%
  mutate(q1 = quantile(avg.wages.pct.state, .25),
         q3 = quantile(avg.wages.pct.state, .75),
         iqr = IQR(avg.wages.pct.state),
         low.outlier = q1 - 1.5*iqr,
         high.outlier = q3 + 1.5*iqr) %>%
  filter(avg.wages.pct.state > low.outlier,
         avg.wages.pct.state < high.outlier) %>%
  ungroup()

labels <- data %>%
  group_by(grad.year.1) %>%
  summarize(Min = quantile(avg.wages.pct.state, .0),
            Q1 = quantile(avg.wages.pct.state, .25),
            Median = median(avg.wages.pct.state),
            Q3 = quantile(avg.wages.pct.state, .75),
            Max = quantile(avg.wages.pct.state, 1),
            Mean = mean(avg.wages.pct.state)) %>%
  ungroup()

plot <- ggplot(data, aes(grad.year.1, avg.wages.pct.state, fill = grad.year.1, group = grad.year.1)) +
  geom_boxplot_interactive() +
  geom_label(data = labels, aes(x = grad.year.1, y = 1.25, label = paste("Min: ", comma(Min, accuracy = .1), "\nQ1: ", comma(Q1, accuracy = .1), "\nMedian: ", comma(Median, accuracy = .1), "\nQ3: ", comma(Q3, accuracy = .1), "\nMax: ", comma(Max, accuracy = ), "\nMean: ", comma(Mean, accuracy = .1))), color = "white") +
  labs(x="", y = "", color="", title = "Boxplots of wages as percent of state wages by employment state")+
  scale_y_continuous(labels=scales::comma,
                     limits = c(0, 1.5))+
  scale_fill_manual(values = brewer.pal(n = 6, "RdYlBu")) +
  theme_bar+
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        text = element_text(size = 18),
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 25, hjust = .9))


girafe(ggobj = plot, width_svg = 10, height_svg = 10) %>%
  girafe_options(opts_selection(type = "none"),
                 opts_sizing(rescale = FALSE)) 
summary <- aov(avg.wages.pct.state ~ grad.year.1, data = data)

summary

tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/Claude/analysis_local_employment_ct/High school characteristics/avg-wages-pct-state-1.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)
data <- meaningful.emp.sw %>%
  select(grad.year.5, avg.wages.pct.state) %>%
  filter(grad.year.5 != "After 2023") %>%
  drop_na() %>%
  group_by(grad.year.5) %>%
  mutate(q1 = quantile(avg.wages.pct.state, .25),
         q3 = quantile(avg.wages.pct.state, .75),
         iqr = IQR(avg.wages.pct.state),
         low.outlier = q1 - 1.5*iqr,
         high.outlier = q3 + 1.5*iqr) %>%
  filter(avg.wages.pct.state > low.outlier,
         avg.wages.pct.state < high.outlier) %>%
  ungroup()

labels <- data %>%
  group_by(grad.year.5) %>%
  summarize(Min = quantile(avg.wages.pct.state, .0),
            Q1 = quantile(avg.wages.pct.state, .25),
            Median = median(avg.wages.pct.state),
            Q3 = quantile(avg.wages.pct.state, .75),
            Max = quantile(avg.wages.pct.state, 1),
            Mean = mean(avg.wages.pct.state)) %>%
  ungroup()

plot <- ggplot(data, aes(grad.year.5, avg.wages.pct.state, fill = grad.year.5, group = grad.year.5)) +
  geom_boxplot_interactive() +
  geom_label(data = labels, aes(x = grad.year.5, y = 1.25, label = paste("Min: ", comma(Min, accuracy = .1), "\nQ1: ", comma(Q1, accuracy = .1), "\nMedian: ", comma(Median, accuracy = .1), "\nQ3: ", comma(Q3, accuracy = .1), "\nMax: ", comma(Max, accuracy = ), "\nMean: ", comma(Mean, accuracy = .1))), color = "white") +
  labs(x="", y = "", color="", title = "Boxplots of wages as percent of state wages by employment state")+
  scale_y_continuous(labels=scales::comma,
                     limits = c(0, 1.5))+
  scale_fill_manual(values = brewer.pal(n = 6, "RdYlBu")) +
  theme_bar+
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        text = element_text(size = 18),
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 25, hjust = .9))


girafe(ggobj = plot, width_svg = 10, height_svg = 10) %>%
  girafe_options(opts_selection(type = "none"),
                 opts_sizing(rescale = FALSE)) 
summary <- aov(avg.wages.pct.state ~ grad.year.5, data = data)

summary
Call:
   aov(formula = avg.wages.pct.state ~ grad.year.5, data = data)

Terms:
                grad.year.5 Residuals
Sum of Squares       0.6555  150.7783
Deg. of Freedom           4     35151

Residual standard error: 0.06549385
Estimated effects may be unbalanced
anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)
  Tukey multiple comparisons of means
    95% family-wise confidence level

Fit: aov(formula = avg.wages.pct.state ~ grad.year.5, data = data)

data <- meaningful.emp.sw %>%

  filter(grad.year.10 != "After 2023") %>%
  drop_na() %>%
  group_by(grad.year.10) %>%
  mutate(q1 = quantile(avg.wages.pct.state, .25),
         q3 = quantile(avg.wages.pct.state, .75),
         iqr = IQR(avg.wages.pct.state),
         low.outlier = q1 - 1.5*iqr,
         high.outlier = q3 + 1.5*iqr) %>%
  filter(avg.wages.pct.state > low.outlier,
         avg.wages.pct.state < high.outlier) %>%
  ungroup()

labels <- data %>%
  group_by(grad.year.10) %>%
  summarize(Min = quantile(avg.wages.pct.state, .0),
            Q1 = quantile(avg.wages.pct.state, .25),
            Median = median(avg.wages.pct.state),
            Q3 = quantile(avg.wages.pct.state, .75),

  geom_boxplot_interactive() +
  geom_label(data = labels, aes(x = grad.year.10, y = 1.25, label = paste("Min: ", comma(Min, accuracy = .1), "\nQ1: ", comma(Q1, accuracy = .1), "\nMedian: ", comma(Median, accuracy = .1), "\nQ3: ", comma(Q3, accuracy = .1), "\nMax: ", comma(Max, accuracy = ), "\nMean: ", comma(Mean, accuracy = .1))), color = "white") +
  labs(x="", y = "", color="", title = "Boxplots of wages as percent of state wages by employment state")+
  scale_y_continuous(labels=scales::comma,
                     limits = c(0, 1.5))+
  scale_fill_manual(values = brewer.pal(n = 6, "RdYlBu")) +
  theme_bar+

        text = element_text(size = 18),
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 25, hjust = .9))

High School Accomplishments

We are going to analyze the following variables;

  • High school accomplishments
    • pseo.participant
    • took.ACT
    • ACTCompositeScore
    • ap.exam
    • MCA scores
      • MCA.M
      • MCA.R
      • MCA.S
    • cte.achievement
    • total.cte.courses.taken


Summary of High School Accomplishment Variables and Workforce Participation Outcomes

The following high school accomplishment variables were examined in relation to workforce participation outcomes at 1, 5, and 10 years after high school graduation. Categorical variables were examined using cross-tabulation and Cramer’s V, while continuous variables were examined using one-way ANOVA with eta-squared as the measure of effect size. Variables are presented in two separate ranked lists reflecting their respective effect size measures.

Categorical Variables - Ranked by Cramer’s V at Year 1

  1. ACT Participation (Cramer’s V: 0.430, 0.225, 0.137) The strongest categorical predictor at year 1 and the strongest overall association observed across all variable groups examined. Students who did not take the ACT transition directly into the workforce at much higher rates and show stronger and persistent attachment to regional employment across all time points, while ACT takers are strongly associated with postsecondary attendance and subsequent out-of-region employment.

  2. PSEO Participation (Cramer’s V: 0.370, 0.165, 0.087) The second strongest categorical predictor at year 1, largely reflecting the direct relationship between PSEO participation and continued postsecondary attendance. PSEO participants show a persistent tendency toward meaningful Minnesota employment over regional employment at years 5 and 10, suggesting PSEO participation is associated with broader geographic mobility in long-term workforce outcomes.

  3. AP Exam Participation (Cramer’s V: 0.217, 0.146, 0.089) A moderate association at year 1 that weakens over time, closely mirroring the ACT participation pattern. AP exam participants show the highest postsecondary attendance rate of any subgroup examined (81.4% at year 1) and a persistent tendency toward meaningful Minnesota employment over regional employment at years 5 and 10.

  4. CTE Achievement (Cramer’s V: 0.075, 0.090, 0.088) The weakest categorical predictor at year 1 but with a remarkably stable and substantively important pattern across all three time points. CTE Concentrators or Completors show the highest rates of meaningful regional employment at every time point, with a clear dose-response relationship across CTE engagement levels. No CTE students show strikingly high rates of no Minnesota employment record by year 10 (43.8%), suggesting CTE participation is strongly associated with long-term regional retention.

Continuous Variables - Ranked by Eta-Squared at Year 1

  1. MCA Math (η²: 0.168, 0.025, 0.017) The strongest continuous predictor at year 1 with a large effect size, the largest observed for any continuous variable examined. A distinctive early pattern shows both not meaningful employment and no Minnesota employment record groups scoring significantly lower than meaningful regional employment — unique among MCA subjects at year 1. By year 10 the meaningful regional employment group shows the lowest Math scores of all groups, indistinguishable from not meaningful employment.

  2. MCA Reading (η²: 0.129, 0.023, 0.057) The second strongest continuous predictor at year 1 with a large effect size, notable for its unique trajectory — weakening to a small effect at year 5 before strengthening to a medium effect at year 10, the only continuous variable to show this pattern. By year 10 the meaningful regional employment group is indistinguishable from not meaningful employment in Reading scores, with both groups significantly below meaningful Minnesota employment and no Minnesota employment record.

  3. MCA Science (η²: 0.085, 0.033, 0.019) A large effect at year 1 that weakens to a small effect over time. The voluntary nature of Science MCA participation creates a self-selected sample of higher-achieving students, which should be considered when interpreting results. The pattern mirrors Reading and Math, with the meaningful regional employment group consistently showing the lowest Science scores and being indistinguishable from not meaningful employment across all time points.

  4. ACT Composite Score (η²: 0.093, 0.038, 0.028) A moderate effect at year 1 that weakens to a small effect over time. The attending postsecondary group shows substantially higher ACT scores than all workforce-engaged groups throughout, and the meaningful regional employment group shows progressively lower ACT scores relative to other groups, with the no Minnesota employment record group consistently showing higher scores than not meaningful employment at years 5 and 10.

  5. Total CTE Courses Taken (η²: 0.032, 0.027, 0.028) A small but remarkably stable effect across all three time points, unique among continuous variables in showing no meaningful decline over time. The meaningful regional employment group shows the highest number of CTE courses taken at every time point, with the gap between regional and out-of-region employment groups growing from no significant difference at year 1 to approximately 1.3 courses by year 10.

  6. Average Wages as Percentage of State Average (η²: 0.010, 0.004, 0.006) A negligible effect across all three time points, explaining at most 1% of variance in workforce participation outcomes. Despite the very small effect size, a consistent finding is that meaningful regional employment shows higher relative wages than meaningful Minnesota employment across all time points, suggesting Southwest Minnesota offers competitive wages relative to the broader state labor market.

  7. Average Unemployment Rate (η²: 0.005, 0.015, 0.032) The weakest predictor at year 1 but showing a growing association over time, reaching a small effect size by year 10. The most consistent finding is that individuals with no Minnesota employment record experience lower average unemployment rates, likely reflecting out-of-state migration to stronger labor markets.

Overall Pattern A clear and consistent theme emerges across the high school accomplishment variables that has direct implications for regional workforce development. Academic achievement indicators — ACT participation, AP exam, PSEO, and all three MCA subjects — are associated with postsecondary attendance and subsequent employment outside the region, with higher achievers increasingly gravitating toward meaningful Minnesota employment or out-of-state migration over time. CTE engagement indicators tell the opposite story — both CTE achievement level and total CTE courses taken are consistently associated with stronger and more persistent regional workforce attachment across all three time points. This divergence between academic and CTE pathways is one of the most substantively important findings of the entire analysis, suggesting that CTE programming plays a meaningful role in regional workforce retention in Southwest Minnesota even if the statistical effect sizes are modest.


pseo.participant

Overview Cross-tabulation analysis examined the relationship between PSEO participation (0 = did not participate, 1 = participated) and workforce participation outcomes at 1, 5, and 10 years after high school graduation. Analysis is restricted to individuals with known PSEO participation status, excluding the 2008-2010 graduation cohorts where PSEO data was unavailable. PSEO participation shows the strongest association of any high school accomplishment variable at year 1, with the association weakening considerably over time.

Year 1 At one year post-graduation, PSEO participation shows a moderate to strong association with workforce participation outcomes (Cramer’s V=0.370), the strongest association observed for any variable examined thus far. PSEO participants are far more likely to be attending postsecondary education (77.7% vs 40.8%), while non-participants show substantially higher rates of meaningful regional employment (17.1% vs 7.5%), not meaningful employment (21.6% vs 8.4%), and no Minnesota employment record (15.0% vs 3.6%). This pattern strongly reflects the design of PSEO — participants are by definition already engaged in postsecondary education while still in high school and are therefore much more likely to continue into postsecondary after graduation. (χ²=5121.797, df=4, Cramer’s V=0.370, p<0.001)

Year 5 By five years post-graduation the association weakens substantially (Cramer’s V=0.165). PSEO participants now show higher rates of meaningful Minnesota employment (21.6% vs 16.5%) and postsecondary attendance (21.8% vs 11.8%), while non-participants show higher rates of meaningful regional employment (26.1% vs 20.2%) and no Minnesota employment record (26.0% vs 21.7%). The reversal in meaningful regional versus Minnesota employment rates between the two groups is notable, suggesting PSEO participants are more likely to find employment outside the region by year 5. (χ²=680.833, df=4, Cramer’s V=0.165, p<0.001)

Year 10 At ten years post-graduation the association weakens considerably (Cramer’s V=0.087). PSEO participants show a notably higher rate of meaningful Minnesota employment (27.8% vs 20.6%), while non-participants show a higher rate of meaningful regional employment (25.0% vs 21.2%). Rates of no Minnesota employment record converge almost entirely between the two groups (37.4% vs 38.0%), suggesting that long-term out-of-state migration patterns are similar regardless of PSEO participation. (χ²=74.460, df=3, Cramer’s V=0.087, p<0.001)

Summary PSEO participation shows the strongest association with workforce participation outcomes at year 1 of any variable examined (Cramer’s V=0.370), largely reflecting the direct relationship between PSEO participation and continued postsecondary attendance. As this immediate effect dissipates, the association weakens considerably by years 5 and 10. A consistent and substantively important pattern emerges over time — PSEO participants are more likely to find meaningful employment outside the region while non-participants show stronger attachment to regional employment, suggesting that PSEO participation may be associated with broader geographic mobility in long-term workforce participation.

  • One year after graduating
  • Five years after graduating
  • Ten years after graduating
data <- meaningful.emp.sw %>%
  select(grad.year.1, pseo.participant) %>%
  filter(grad.year.1 != "After 2023",
         pseo.participant != is.na(pseo.participant)) %>%
  droplevels()

         title = "", 
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/Claude/analysis_local_employment_ct/High school accomplishments/pseo-participant-1.html")

took.ACT

Overview Cross-tabulation analysis examined the relationship between ACT participation (No = did not take ACT, Yes = took ACT) and workforce participation outcomes at 1, 5, and 10 years after high school graduation. ACT participation shows the strongest association of any variable examined thus far at year 1, with the association weakening considerably but remaining meaningful through year 10.

Year 1 At one year post-graduation, ACT participation shows a strong association with workforce participation outcomes (Cramer’s V=0.430), the strongest observed for any variable examined across all groups. Students who took the ACT are far more likely to be attending postsecondary education (71.3% vs 27.1%), while non-ACT students show substantially higher rates of meaningful regional employment (19.6% vs 9.2%), not meaningful employment (26.6% vs 11.2%), and no Minnesota employment record (20.7% vs 5.1%). This pattern reflects the strong relationship between ACT participation and postsecondary education intentions, with non-ACT students transitioning directly into the workforce at much higher rates. (χ²=8821.737, df=4, Cramer’s V=0.430, p<0.001)

Year 5 By five years post-graduation the association weakens substantially but remains moderate (Cramer’s V=0.225). ACT participants show notably higher rates of meaningful Minnesota employment (20.6% vs 13.6%) and postsecondary attendance (21.9% vs 7.3%), while non-ACT students show higher rates of meaningful regional employment (28.6% vs 21.0%) and no Minnesota employment record (28.9% vs 20.7%). The divergence between the two groups in meaningful regional versus Minnesota employment is striking, with ACT participants considerably more likely to find employment outside the region. (χ²=1789.877, df=4, Cramer’s V=0.225, p<0.001)

Year 10 At ten years post-graduation the association weakens further but remains weak to moderate (Cramer’s V=0.137). ACT participants continue to show a notably higher rate of meaningful Minnesota employment (27.8% vs 17.1%), while non-ACT students maintain a higher rate of meaningful regional employment (28.3% vs 22.5%). Rates of no Minnesota employment record converge almost entirely between the two groups (36.1% vs 36.2%), suggesting that long-term out-of-state migration is similar regardless of ACT participation. (χ²=375.583, df=3, Cramer’s V=0.137, p<0.001)

Summary ACT participation shows the strongest association with workforce participation outcomes at year 1 of any variable examined (Cramer’s V=0.430), primarily reflecting the strong relationship between ACT participation and postsecondary education intentions. As this immediate effect dissipates, the association weakens considerably but remains meaningful through year 10. A consistent and substantively important pattern emerges across all three time points — ACT participants are considerably more likely to find meaningful employment outside the region while non-ACT students show stronger and persistent attachment to regional employment. This geographic sorting effect is one of the most pronounced observed across all variable groups examined.

  • One year after graduating
  • Five years after graduating
  • Ten years after graduating
data <- meaningful.emp.sw %>%
  select(grad.year.1, took.ACT) %>%
  filter(grad.year.1 != "After 2023") %>%
  droplevels()

tab_xtab(var.row = data$grad.year.1, var.col = data$took.ACT, 
         title = "", 
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/Claude/analysis_local_employment_ct/High school accomplishments/took-ACT-1.html")
