χ2=375.583 · df=3 · Cramer's V=0.137 · p=0.000


ACTCompositeScore

Overview One-way ANOVA examined differences in ACT composite score across workforce participation outcome groups at 1, 5, and 10 years after high school graduation, restricted to individuals who took the ACT. While statistically significant at all three time points, eta-squared values indicate a moderate effect at year 1 that weakens over time, suggesting that ACT composite score explains a meaningful proportion of variance in workforce participation outcomes particularly in the early post-graduation period.

Year 1 At one year post-graduation, ACT composite score differs significantly across outcome groups (F=799.65, df=4, p<0.001) with a moderate effect size (η²=0.093). The attending postsecondary group shows substantially higher ACT scores than all other groups (estimate=3.50 vs meaningful regional employment, p<0.001), reflecting the strong relationship between academic achievement and postsecondary attendance. The meaningful Minnesota employment group shows significantly higher scores than the meaningful regional employment group (estimate=0.84, p<0.001). No significant difference is found between the meaningful regional employment, not meaningful employment, and no Minnesota employment record groups, suggesting ACT score does not meaningfully distinguish between these three non-postsecondary outcomes at year 1.

Year 5 By five years post-graduation the association weakens but remains a small to moderate effect (F=225.44, df=4, p<0.001, η²=0.038). The attending postsecondary group continues to show the highest ACT scores, significantly higher than all other groups. The meaningful Minnesota employment group now shows significantly higher scores than the meaningful regional employment group (estimate=1.84, p<0.001). Notably, the no Minnesota employment record group shows significantly higher scores than the not meaningful employment group (estimate=0.94, p<0.001), suggesting that individuals who leave Minnesota without a record tend to have higher academic achievement than those who remain with unstable employment.

Year 10 At ten years post-graduation the association weakens further to a small effect (F=114.55, df=3, p<0.001, η²=0.028). The meaningful regional employment group now shows the lowest ACT scores of all groups, significantly lower than meaningful Minnesota employment (estimate=1.60, p<0.001), not meaningful employment (estimate=1.02, p<0.001), and no Minnesota employment record (estimate=1.71, p<0.001). The no Minnesota employment record group shows significantly higher scores than the not meaningful employment group (estimate=0.68, p<0.001), consistent with the pattern observed at year 5.

Summary ACT composite score shows a moderate association with workforce participation outcomes at year 1 (η²=0.093) that weakens to a small effect by years 5 and 10 (η²=0.038, 0.028). The most consistent and substantively important finding is that the attending postsecondary group shows substantially higher ACT scores than all workforce-engaged groups, and that the meaningful regional employment group shows progressively lower ACT scores relative to other groups over time. By year 10 the meaningful regional employment group shows the lowest ACT scores of any group, suggesting that higher academic achievement is associated with employment outside the region in the long term.

  • One year after graduating
  • Five years after graduating
  • Ten years after graduating
data <- meaningful.emp.sw %>%
  select(grad.year.1, ACTCompositeScore) %>%
  filter(grad.year.1 != "After 2023") %>%
  drop_na() %>%
  group_by(grad.year.1) %>%
  mutate(q1 = quantile(ACTCompositeScore, .25),
         q3 = quantile(ACTCompositeScore, .75),
         iqr = IQR(ACTCompositeScore),
         low.outlier = q1 - 1.5*iqr,
         high.outlier = q3 + 1.5*iqr) %>%
  filter(ACTCompositeScore > low.outlier,
         ACTCompositeScore < high.outlier) %>%
  ungroup()

labels <- data %>%
  group_by(grad.year.1) %>%
  summarize(Min = quantile(ACTCompositeScore, .0),
            Q1 = quantile(ACTCompositeScore, .25),
            Median = median(ACTCompositeScore),
            Q3 = quantile(ACTCompositeScore, .75),
            Max = quantile(ACTCompositeScore, 1),
            Mean = mean(ACTCompositeScore)) %>%
  ungroup()

plot <- ggplot(data, aes(grad.year.1, ACTCompositeScore, fill = grad.year.1, group = grad.year.1)) +
  geom_boxplot_interactive() +
  geom_label(data = labels, aes(x = grad.year.1, y = 40, label = paste("Min: ", comma(Min, accuracy = .1), "\nQ1: ", comma(Q1, accuracy = .1), "\nMedian: ", comma(Median, accuracy = .1), "\nQ3: ", comma(Q3, accuracy = .1), "\nMax: ", comma(Max, accuracy = ), "\nMean: ", comma(Mean, accuracy = .1))), color = "white") +
  labs(x="", y = "", color="", title = "Boxplots of ACT composite score by employment state")+
  scale_y_continuous(labels=scales::comma,
                     limits = c(0, 45))+
  scale_fill_manual(values = brewer.pal(n = 6, "RdYlBu")) +
  theme_bar+
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        text = element_text(size = 18),
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 25, hjust = .9))


girafe(ggobj = plot, width_svg = 10, height_svg = 10) %>%
  girafe_options(opts_selection(type = "none"),
                 opts_sizing(rescale = FALSE)) 
summary <- aov(ACTCompositeScore ~ grad.year.1, data = data)

summary
Call:
   aov(formula = ACTCompositeScore ~ grad.year.1, data = data)

Terms:
                grad.year.1 Residuals
Sum of Squares      57979.6  568645.0
Deg. of Freedom           4     31371

Residual standard error: 4.257517
Estimated effects may be unbalanced
anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)
  Tukey multiple comparisons of means
    95% family-wise confidence level

Fit: aov(formula = ACTCompositeScore ~ grad.year.1, data = data)

tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/Claude/analysis_local_employment_ct/High school accomplishments/ACTCompositeScore-1.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)
data <- meaningful.emp.sw %>%
  select(grad.year.5, ACTCompositeScore) %>%
  filter(grad.year.5 != "After 2023") %>%
  drop_na() %>%
  group_by(grad.year.5) %>%
  mutate(q1 = quantile(ACTCompositeScore, .25),
         q3 = quantile(ACTCompositeScore, .75),
         iqr = IQR(ACTCompositeScore),
         low.outlier = q1 - 1.5*iqr,
         high.outlier = q3 + 1.5*iqr) %>%
  filter(ACTCompositeScore > low.outlier,
         ACTCompositeScore < high.outlier) %>%
  ungroup()

labels <- data %>%
  group_by(grad.year.5) %>%

            Q1 = quantile(ACTCompositeScore, .25),
            Median = median(ACTCompositeScore),
            Q3 = quantile(ACTCompositeScore, .75),
            Max = quantile(ACTCompositeScore, 1),
            Mean = mean(ACTCompositeScore)) %>%
  ungroup()

  geom_boxplot_interactive() +
  geom_label(data = labels, aes(x = grad.year.5, y = 40, label = paste("Min: ", comma(Min, accuracy = .1), "\nQ1: ", comma(Q1, accuracy = .1), "\nMedian: ", comma(Median, accuracy = .1), "\nQ3: ", comma(Q3, accuracy = .1), "\nMax: ", comma(Max, accuracy = ), "\nMean: ", comma(Mean, accuracy = .1))), color = "white") +

ap.exam

Overview Cross-tabulation analysis examined the relationship between AP exam participation (0 = did not take AP exam, 1 = took AP exam) and workforce participation outcomes at 1, 5, and 10 years after high school graduation. AP exam participation shows a moderate association at year 1 that weakens considerably over time, with a pattern broadly similar to ACT participation but somewhat weaker throughout.

Year 1 At one year post-graduation, AP exam participation shows a moderate association with workforce participation outcomes (Cramer’s V=0.217). AP exam participants are far more likely to be attending postsecondary education (81.4% vs 51.9%), the highest postsecondary attendance rate observed for any subgroup across all variables examined. Non-AP exam students show substantially higher rates of meaningful regional employment (14.1% vs 5.2%), not meaningful employment (17.9% vs 7.8%), and no Minnesota employment record (11.6% vs 3.5%), reflecting the strong relationship between AP exam participation and postsecondary education intentions. (χ²=2239.394, df=4, Cramer’s V=0.217, p<0.001)

Year 5 By five years post-graduation the association weakens considerably (Cramer’s V=0.146). AP exam participants show notably higher rates of postsecondary attendance (26.7% vs 15.1%) and meaningful Minnesota employment (23.2% vs 17.3%), while non-AP exam students show higher rates of meaningful regional employment (25.3% vs 14.3%). The divergence in meaningful regional versus Minnesota employment between the two groups is pronounced, mirroring the pattern observed for ACT participation and suggesting AP exam participants are more likely to find employment outside the region. (χ²=751.737, df=4, Cramer’s V=0.146, p<0.001)

Year 10 At ten years post-graduation the association weakens further (Cramer’s V=0.089). AP exam participants continue to show a notably higher rate of meaningful Minnesota employment (30.3% vs 22.2%), while non-AP exam students maintain a higher rate of meaningful regional employment (26.0% vs 18.3%). Rates of no Minnesota employment record are similar between the two groups (38.4% vs 35.8%), suggesting long-term out-of-state migration patterns converge regardless of AP exam participation. (χ²=159.083, df=3, Cramer’s V=0.089, p<0.001)

Summary AP exam participation shows a moderate association with workforce participation outcomes at year 1 (Cramer’s V=0.217) that weakens considerably over time, reaching weak levels by year 10 (Cramer’s V=0.089). The pattern closely mirrors that of ACT participation, with AP exam participants showing stronger attachment to postsecondary education at year 1 and a persistent tendency toward meaningful Minnesota employment over the regional labor market at years 5 and 10. Non-AP exam students show a consistent and growing advantage in meaningful regional employment across all time points, reinforcing the broader pattern observed across high school accomplishment variables that higher academic achievement is associated with employment outside the region.

  • One year after graduating
  • Five years after graduating
  • Ten years after graduating
data <- meaningful.emp.sw %>%
  select(grad.year.1, ap.exam) %>%
  filter(grad.year.1 != "After 2023") %>%
  droplevels()

tab_xtab(var.row = data$grad.year.1, var.col = data$ap.exam, 
         title = "", 
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/Claude/analysis_local_employment_ct/High school accomplishments/ap-exam-1.html")

χ2=159.083 · df=3 · Cramer's V=0.089 · p=0.000


MCA.R

Overview One-way ANOVA examined differences in MCA Reading scores across workforce participation outcome groups at 1, 5, and 10 years after high school graduation. Note that 2,698 individuals (5.3%) are missing MCA Reading scores and are excluded from this analysis. eta-squared values indicate a large effect at year 1 that weakens considerably by year 5 before strengthening again at year 10, suggesting a complex and evolving relationship between Reading achievement and workforce participation outcomes over time.

Year 1 At one year post-graduation, MCA Reading score differs significantly across outcome groups (F=1633.60, df=4, p<0.001) with a large effect size (η²=0.129). The attending postsecondary group shows the highest Reading scores, significantly higher than all other groups (estimate=0.625 vs meaningful regional employment, p<0.001). The meaningful Minnesota employment group shows significantly higher scores than meaningful regional employment (estimate=0.361, p<0.001). The no Minnesota employment record group shows significantly lower scores than all other groups except not meaningful employment, while no significant difference is found between meaningful regional employment and not meaningful employment groups.

Year 5 By five years post-graduation the association weakens considerably to a small effect (F=180.08, df=4, p<0.001, η²=0.023). The attending postsecondary group continues to show the highest Reading scores, significantly higher than all other groups. The meaningful Minnesota employment group shows significantly higher scores than meaningful regional employment (estimate=0.221, p<0.001), while the no Minnesota employment record group now shows significantly higher scores than both meaningful regional employment (estimate=0.163, p<0.001) and not meaningful employment (estimate=0.111, p<0.001), suggesting that individuals who leave Minnesota tend to have higher Reading achievement than those remaining in unstable regional employment.

Year 10 At ten years post-graduation the association strengthens notably (F=362.29, df=3, p<0.001, η²=0.057), reaching a medium effect size — the only ANOVA variable to strengthen to this degree at year 10. The meaningful regional employment group shows the lowest Reading scores of all groups, significantly lower than meaningful Minnesota employment (estimate=0.431, p<0.001) and no Minnesota employment record (estimate=0.418, p<0.001). No significant difference is found between the meaningful regional employment and not meaningful employment groups, nor between meaningful Minnesota employment and no Minnesota employment record groups, suggesting that by year 10 Reading achievement primarily distinguishes regional employment from all other outcomes rather than differentiating among non-regional outcomes.

Summary MCA Reading score shows a large association with workforce participation outcomes at year 1 (η²=0.129) that weakens to a small effect at year 5 (η²=0.023) before strengthening to a medium effect at year 10 (η²=0.057) — a unique trajectory among all variables examined. The most substantively important finding is that the meaningful regional employment group shows progressively lower Reading scores relative to other groups over time, becoming indistinguishable from the not meaningful employment group by year 10. This suggests that Reading achievement is strongly associated with long-term employment outside the region, and that regional employment increasingly attracts individuals with lower Reading achievement profiles as careers develop.

  • One year after graduating
  • Five years after graduating
  • Ten years after graduating
data <- meaningful.emp.sw %>%
  select(grad.year.1, MCA.R) %>%
  filter(grad.year.1 != "After 2023") %>%
  drop_na() %>%
  group_by(grad.year.1) %>%
  mutate(q1 = quantile(MCA.R, .25),
         q3 = quantile(MCA.R, .75),
         iqr = IQR(MCA.R),
         low.outlier = q1 - 1.5*iqr,
         high.outlier = q3 + 1.5*iqr) %>%
  filter(MCA.R > low.outlier,
         MCA.R < high.outlier) %>%
  ungroup()

labels <- data %>%
  group_by(grad.year.1) %>%
  summarize(Min = quantile(MCA.R, .0),
            Q1 = quantile(MCA.R, .25),
            Median = median(MCA.R),
            Q3 = quantile(MCA.R, .75),
            Max = quantile(MCA.R, 1),
            Mean = mean(MCA.R)) %>%
  ungroup()

plot <- ggplot(data, aes(grad.year.1, MCA.R, fill = grad.year.1, group = grad.year.1)) +
  geom_boxplot_interactive() +
  geom_label(data = labels, aes(x = grad.year.1, y = 5, label = paste("Min: ", comma(Min, accuracy = .1), "\nQ1: ", comma(Q1, accuracy = .1), "\nMedian: ", comma(Median, accuracy = .1), "\nQ3: ", comma(Q3, accuracy = .1), "\nMax: ", comma(Max, accuracy = ), "\nMean: ", comma(Mean, accuracy = .1))), color = "white") +
  labs(x="", y = "", color="", title = "Boxplots of MCA-Reading score by employment state")+
  scale_y_continuous(labels=scales::comma,
                     limits = c(0, 6))+
  scale_fill_manual(values = brewer.pal(n = 6, "RdYlBu")) +
  theme_bar+
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        text = element_text(size = 18),
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 25, hjust = .9))


girafe(ggobj = plot, width_svg = 10, height_svg = 10) %>%
  girafe_options(opts_selection(type = "none"),
                 opts_sizing(rescale = FALSE)) 
summary <- aov(MCA.R ~ grad.year.1, data = data)

summary
Call:
   aov(formula = MCA.R ~ grad.year.1, data = data)

Terms:
                grad.year.1 Residuals
Sum of Squares     4325.108 29243.308
Deg. of Freedom           4     44181

Residual standard error: 0.813571
Estimated effects may be unbalanced
anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)
  Tukey multiple comparisons of means
    95% family-wise confidence level

Fit: aov(formula = MCA.R ~ grad.year.1, data = data)

tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/Claude/analysis_local_employment_ct/High school accomplishments/MCA-R-1.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)
data <- meaningful.emp.sw %>%
  select(grad.year.5, MCA.R) %>%
  filter(grad.year.5 != "After 2023") %>%
  drop_na() %>%
  group_by(grad.year.5) %>%
  mutate(q1 = quantile(MCA.R, .25),
         q3 = quantile(MCA.R, .75),
         iqr = IQR(MCA.R),
         low.outlier = q1 - 1.5*iqr,
         high.outlier = q3 + 1.5*iqr) %>%
  filter(MCA.R > low.outlier,
         MCA.R < high.outlier) %>%
  ungroup()

labels <- data %>%
  group_by(grad.year.5) %>%
  summarize(Min = quantile(MCA.R, .0),
            Q1 = quantile(MCA.R, .25),
            Median = median(MCA.R),
            Q3 = quantile(MCA.R, .75),
            Max = quantile(MCA.R, 1),
            Mean = mean(MCA.R)) %>%
  ungroup()

plot <- ggplot(data, aes(grad.year.5, MCA.R, fill = grad.year.5, group = grad.year.5)) +
  geom_boxplot_interactive() +
  geom_label(data = labels, aes(x = grad.year.5, y = 5, label = paste("Min: ", comma(Min, accuracy = .1), "\nQ1: ", comma(Q1, accuracy = .1), "\nMedian: ", comma(Median, accuracy = .1), "\nQ3: ", comma(Q3, accuracy = .1), "\nMax: ", comma(Max, accuracy = ), "\nMean: ", comma(Mean, accuracy = .1))), color = "white") +
  labs(x="", y = "", color="", title = "Boxplots of MCA-Reading score by employment state")+
  scale_y_continuous(labels=scales::comma,
                     limits = c(0, 6))+
  scale_fill_manual(values = brewer.pal(n = 6, "RdYlBu")) +
  theme_bar+
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        text = element_text(size = 18),
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 25, hjust = .9))

  select(grad.year.10, MCA.R) %>%
  filter(grad.year.10 != "After 2023") %>%
  drop_na() %>%
  group_by(grad.year.10) %>%
  mutate(q1 = quantile(MCA.R, .25),
         q3 = quantile(MCA.R, .75),
         iqr = IQR(MCA.R),
         low.outlier = q1 - 1.5*iqr,
         high.outlier = q3 + 1.5*iqr) %>%
  filter(MCA.R > low.outlier,
         MCA.R < high.outlier) %>%

            Q1 = quantile(MCA.R, .25),
            Median = median(MCA.R),

            Max = quantile(MCA.R, 1),

  geom_boxplot_interactive() +
  geom_label(data = labels, aes(x = grad.year.10, y = 5, label = paste("Min: ", comma(Min, accuracy = .1), "\nQ1: ", comma(Q1, accuracy = .1), "\nMedian: ", comma(Median, accuracy = .1), "\nQ3: ", comma(Q3, accuracy = .1), "\nMax: ", comma(Max, accuracy = ), "\nMean: ", comma(Mean, accuracy = .1))), color = "white") +

saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/Claude/analysis_local_employment_ct/High school accomplishments/MCA-R-10.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)


MCA.S

Overview One-way ANOVA examined differences in MCA Science scores across workforce participation outcome groups at 1, 5, and 10 years after high school graduation. MCA Science is a voluntary assessment and 9,286 individuals (18.3%) are missing scores, reflecting a combination of data availability limitations and voluntary participation. As discussed in the methodology, individuals who chose to take the Science MCA represent a self-selected group that tends to score higher on Reading and Math assessments, and this self-selection should be considered when interpreting these results. Eta-squared values indicate a large effect at year 1 that weakens considerably over time.

Year 1 At one year post-graduation, MCA Science score differs significantly across outcome groups (F=893.11, df=4, p<0.001) with a large effect size (η²=0.085). The attending postsecondary group shows the highest Science scores, significantly higher than all other groups (estimate=0.519 vs meaningful regional employment, p<0.001). The meaningful Minnesota employment group shows significantly higher scores than the meaningful regional employment group (estimate=0.156, p<0.001). Notably, no significant difference is found between the meaningful regional employment, not meaningful employment, and no Minnesota employment record groups, suggesting that Science achievement does not meaningfully distinguish between these three non-postsecondary outcomes at year 1.

Year 5 By five years post-graduation the association weakens to a small effect (F=231.81, df=4, p<0.001, η²=0.033). The attending postsecondary group continues to show the highest Science scores, significantly higher than all other groups. The meaningful Minnesota employment group shows significantly higher scores than both meaningful regional employment (estimate=0.290, p<0.001) and not meaningful employment (estimate=-0.285, p<0.001). The no Minnesota employment record group now shows significantly higher scores than both meaningful regional employment (estimate=0.176, p<0.001) and not meaningful employment (estimate=0.172, p<0.001), suggesting that higher Science achievement is associated with both out-of-region Minnesota employment and out-of-state migration. No significant difference is found between meaningful regional employment and not meaningful employment groups.

Year 10 At ten years post-graduation the association weakens further to a small effect (F=81.06, df=3, p<0.001, η²=0.019). The meaningful regional employment group continues to show the lowest Science scores, significantly lower than meaningful Minnesota employment (estimate=0.250, p<0.001) and no Minnesota employment record (estimate=0.227, p<0.001). No significant difference is found between meaningful regional employment and not meaningful employment groups, nor between meaningful Minnesota employment and no Minnesota employment record groups, mirroring the pattern observed for MCA Reading at year 10.

Summary MCA Science score shows a large association with workforce participation outcomes at year 1 (η²=0.085) that weakens to a small effect by years 5 and 10 (η²=0.033, 0.019). The pattern closely parallels MCA Reading and Math, with the meaningful regional employment group consistently showing the lowest Science scores and being indistinguishable from not meaningful employment across all time points. The absence of significant differences between meaningful regional employment and not meaningful employment groups across all three time points is a notable finding, suggesting that Science achievement primarily distinguishes regional employment from out-of-region outcomes rather than from unstable employment. Given the voluntary nature of Science MCA participation, these findings should be interpreted with the understanding that they reflect a self-selected, higher-achieving subset of the overall population.

  • One year after graduating
  • Five years after graduating
  • Ten years after graduating
data <- meaningful.emp.sw %>%
  select(grad.year.1, MCA.S) %>%
  filter(grad.year.1 != "After 2023") %>%
  drop_na() %>%
  group_by(grad.year.1) %>%
  mutate(q1 = quantile(MCA.S, .25),
         q3 = quantile(MCA.S, .75),
         iqr = IQR(MCA.S),
         low.outlier = q1 - 1.5*iqr,
         high.outlier = q3 + 1.5*iqr) %>%
  filter(MCA.S > low.outlier,
         MCA.S < high.outlier) %>%
  ungroup()

labels <- data %>%
  group_by(grad.year.1) %>%
  summarize(Min = quantile(MCA.S, .0),
            Q1 = quantile(MCA.S, .25),
            Median = median(MCA.S),
            Q3 = quantile(MCA.S, .75),
            Max = quantile(MCA.S, 1),
            Mean = mean(MCA.S)) %>%
  ungroup()

plot <- ggplot(data, aes(grad.year.1, MCA.S, fill = grad.year.1, group = grad.year.1)) +
  geom_boxplot_interactive() +
  geom_label(data = labels, aes(x = grad.year.1, y = 5, label = paste("Min: ", comma(Min, accuracy = .1), "\nQ1: ", comma(Q1, accuracy = .1), "\nMedian: ", comma(Median, accuracy = .1), "\nQ3: ", comma(Q3, accuracy = .1), "\nMax: ", comma(Max, accuracy = ), "\nMean: ", comma(Mean, accuracy = .1))), color = "white") +
  labs(x="", y = "", color="", title = "Boxplots of MCA-Science score by employment state")+
  scale_y_continuous(labels=scales::comma,
                     limits = c(0, 6))+
  scale_fill_manual(values = brewer.pal(n = 6, "RdYlBu")) +
  theme_bar+
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        text = element_text(size = 18),
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 25, hjust = .9))


girafe(ggobj = plot, width_svg = 10, height_svg = 10) %>%
  girafe_options(opts_selection(type = "none"),
                 opts_sizing(rescale = FALSE)) 
summary <- aov(MCA.S ~ grad.year.1, data = data)

summary
Call:
   aov(formula = MCA.S ~ grad.year.1, data = data)

Terms:
                grad.year.1 Residuals
Sum of Squares     2593.695 28011.520
Deg. of Freedom           4     38582

Residual standard error: 0.8520714
Estimated effects may be unbalanced
anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)
  Tukey multiple comparisons of means
    95% family-wise confidence level

Fit: aov(formula = MCA.S ~ grad.year.1, data = data)

tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/Claude/analysis_local_employment_ct/High school accomplishments/MCA-S-1.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)

  select(grad.year.10, MCA.S) %>%
  filter(grad.year.10 != "After 2023") %>%
  drop_na() %>%
  group_by(grad.year.10) %>%
  mutate(q1 = quantile(MCA.S, .25),
         q3 = quantile(MCA.S, .75),
         iqr = IQR(MCA.S),
         low.outlier = q1 - 1.5*iqr,
         high.outlier = q3 + 1.5*iqr) %>%
  filter(MCA.S > low.outlier,
         MCA.S < high.outlier) %>%
  ungroup()

labels <- data %>%
  group_by(grad.year.10) %>%

            Q1 = quantile(MCA.S, .25),
            Median = median(MCA.S),
            Q3 = quantile(MCA.S, .75),
            Max = quantile(MCA.S, 1),
            Mean = mean(MCA.S)) %>%
  ungroup()

  geom_boxplot_interactive() +

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/Claude/analysis_local_employment_ct/High school accomplishments/MCA-S-10.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)


MCA.M

Overview One-way ANOVA examined differences in MCA Math scores across workforce participation outcome groups at 1, 5, and 10 years after high school graduation. Note that 2,349 individuals (4.6%) are missing MCA Math scores and are excluded from this analysis. Eta-squared values indicate a large effect at year 1 that weakens considerably over time, with MCA Math showing the strongest effect size of the three MCA subjects at year 1.

Year 1 At one year post-graduation, MCA Math score differs significantly across outcome groups (F=2171.45, df=4, p<0.001) with a large effect size (η²=0.168), the largest observed for any continuous variable examined. The attending postsecondary group shows the highest Math scores, significantly higher than all other groups (estimate=0.722 vs meaningful regional employment, p<0.001) — the largest single group difference observed across all MCA subjects. The meaningful Minnesota employment group shows significantly higher scores than meaningful regional employment (estimate=0.137, p<0.001). Distinctively, both the not meaningful employment (estimate=-0.111, p<0.001) and no Minnesota employment record (estimate=-0.164, p<0.001) groups show significantly lower scores than meaningful regional employment — a pattern unique among the three MCA subjects at year 1 — suggesting that Math achievement is associated with stable employment of any kind in the early post-graduation period.

Year 5 By five years post-graduation the association weakens considerably to a small effect (F=211.84, df=4, p<0.001, η²=0.025). The attending postsecondary group continues to show the highest Math scores, significantly higher than all other groups. The meaningful Minnesota employment group shows significantly higher scores than both meaningful regional employment (estimate=0.314, p<0.001) and not meaningful employment (estimate=-0.352, p<0.001). The no Minnesota employment record group shows significantly higher scores than both meaningful regional employment (estimate=0.144, p<0.001) and not meaningful employment (estimate=0.181, p<0.001), but significantly lower scores than meaningful Minnesota employment (estimate=-0.170, p<0.001). No significant difference is found between meaningful regional employment and not meaningful employment groups.

Year 10 At ten years post-graduation the association remains a small effect (F=111.29, df=3, p<0.001, η²=0.017). The meaningful regional employment group shows the lowest Math scores, significantly lower than meaningful Minnesota employment (estimate=0.317, p<0.001) and no Minnesota employment record (estimate=0.272, p<0.001). No significant difference is found between meaningful regional employment and not meaningful employment groups, nor between meaningful Minnesota employment and no Minnesota employment record groups, consistent with the patterns observed for MCA Reading and Science at year 10.

Summary MCA Math score shows the largest effect size of the three MCA subjects at year 1 (η²=0.168) — a large effect by conventional benchmarks — that weakens to a small effect by years 5 and 10 (η²=0.025, 0.017). A distinctive pattern at year 1 unique among MCA subjects is that both the not meaningful employment and no Minnesota employment record groups score significantly lower than meaningful regional employment, suggesting that Math achievement is uniquely associated with stable employment of any kind in the early post-graduation period. By years 5 and 10 the pattern converges with Reading and Science, with the meaningful regional employment group showing the lowest Math scores and being indistinguishable from not meaningful employment. This consistent long-term pattern across all three MCA subjects strongly suggests that higher academic achievement is associated with employment outside the region, while regional employment increasingly attracts individuals with lower academic achievement profiles over time.

  • One year after graduating
  • Five years after graduating
  • Ten years after graduating
data <- meaningful.emp.sw %>%
  select(grad.year.1, MCA.M) %>%
  filter(grad.year.1 != "After 2023") %>%
  drop_na() %>%
  group_by(grad.year.1) %>%
  mutate(q1 = quantile(MCA.M, .25),
         q3 = quantile(MCA.M, .75),
         iqr = IQR(MCA.M),
         low.outlier = q1 - 1.5*iqr,
         high.outlier = q3 + 1.5*iqr) %>%
  filter(MCA.M > low.outlier,
         MCA.M < high.outlier) %>%
  ungroup()

labels <- data %>%
  group_by(grad.year.1) %>%
  summarize(Min = quantile(MCA.M, .0),
            Q1 = quantile(MCA.M, .25),
            Median = median(MCA.M),
            Q3 = quantile(MCA.M, .75),
            Max = quantile(MCA.M, 1),
            Mean = mean(MCA.M)) %>%
  ungroup()

plot <- ggplot(data, aes(grad.year.1, MCA.M, fill = grad.year.1, group = grad.year.1)) +
  geom_boxplot_interactive() +
  geom_label(data = labels, aes(x = grad.year.1, y = 5, label = paste("Min: ", comma(Min, accuracy = .1), "\nQ1: ", comma(Q1, accuracy = .1), "\nMedian: ", comma(Median, accuracy = .1), "\nQ3: ", comma(Q3, accuracy = .1), "\nMax: ", comma(Max, accuracy = ), "\nMean: ", comma(Mean, accuracy = .1))), color = "white") +
  labs(x="", y = "", color="", title = "Boxplots of MCA-Math score by employment state")+
  scale_y_continuous(labels=scales::comma,
                     limits = c(0, 6))+
  scale_fill_manual(values = brewer.pal(n = 6, "RdYlBu")) +
  theme_bar+
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        text = element_text(size = 18),
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 25, hjust = .9))


girafe(ggobj = plot, width_svg = 10, height_svg = 10) %>%
  girafe_options(opts_selection(type = "none"),
                 opts_sizing(rescale = FALSE))

tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/Claude/analysis_local_employment_ct/High school accomplishments/MCA-M-1.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)
data <- meaningful.emp.sw %>%
  select(grad.year.5, MCA.M) %>%
  filter(grad.year.5 != "After 2023") %>%
  drop_na() %>%
  group_by(grad.year.5) %>%
  mutate(q1 = quantile(MCA.M, .25),
         q3 = quantile(MCA.M, .75),
         iqr = IQR(MCA.M),
         low.outlier = q1 - 1.5*iqr,
         high.outlier = q3 + 1.5*iqr) %>%
  filter(MCA.M > low.outlier,
         MCA.M < high.outlier) %>%
  ungroup()

labels <- data %>%
  group_by(grad.year.5) %>%
  summarize(Min = quantile(MCA.M, .0),
            Q1 = quantile(MCA.M, .25),
            Median = median(MCA.M),
            Q3 = quantile(MCA.M, .75),
            Max = quantile(MCA.M, 1),
            Mean = mean(MCA.M)) %>%
  ungroup()

plot <- ggplot(data, aes(grad.year.5, MCA.M, fill = grad.year.5, group = grad.year.5)) +
  geom_boxplot_interactive() +
  geom_label(data = labels, aes(x = grad.year.5, y = 5, label = paste("Min: ", comma(Min, accuracy = .1), "\nQ1: ", comma(Q1, accuracy = .1), "\nMedian: ", comma(Median, accuracy = .1), "\nQ3: ", comma(Q3, accuracy = .1), "\nMax: ", comma(Max, accuracy = ), "\nMean: ", comma(Mean, accuracy = .1))), color = "white") +
  labs(x="", y = "", color="", title = "Boxplots of MCA-Math score by employment state")+
  scale_y_continuous(labels=scales::comma,
                     limits = c(0, 6))+
  scale_fill_manual(values = brewer.pal(n = 6, "RdYlBu")) +
  theme_bar+
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        text = element_text(size = 18),
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 25, hjust = .9))


girafe(ggobj = plot, width_svg = 10, height_svg = 10) %>%
  girafe_options(opts_selection(type = "none"),
                 opts_sizing(rescale = FALSE)) 
summary <- aov(MCA.M ~ grad.year.5, data = data)

summary

   aov(formula = MCA.M ~ grad.year.5, data = data)

Terms:
                grad.year.5 Residuals
Sum of Squares       918.82  36448.09
Deg. of Freedom           4     33613

Residual standard error: 1.041319
Estimated effects may be unbalanced
anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)

data <- meaningful.emp.sw %>%
  select(grad.year.10, MCA.M) %>%
  filter(grad.year.10 != "After 2023") %>%
  drop_na() %>%
  group_by(grad.year.10) %>%
  mutate(q1 = quantile(MCA.M, .25),
         q3 = quantile(MCA.M, .75),
         iqr = IQR(MCA.M),
         low.outlier = q1 - 1.5*iqr,
         high.outlier = q3 + 1.5*iqr) %>%
  filter(MCA.M > low.outlier,
         MCA.M < high.outlier) %>%
  ungroup()

labels <- data %>%
  group_by(grad.year.10) %>%
  summarize(Min = quantile(MCA.M, .0),
            Q1 = quantile(MCA.M, .25),
            Median = median(MCA.M),
            Q3 = quantile(MCA.M, .75),
            Max = quantile(MCA.M, 1),
            Mean = mean(MCA.M)) %>%
  ungroup()

plot <- ggplot(data, aes(grad.year.10, MCA.M, fill = grad.year.10, group = grad.year.10)) +
  geom_boxplot_interactive() +
  geom_label(data = labels, aes(x = grad.year.10, y = 5, label = paste("Min: ", comma(Min, accuracy = .1), "\nQ1: ", comma(Q1, accuracy = .1), "\nMedian: ", comma(Median, accuracy = .1), "\nQ3: ", comma(Q3, accuracy = .1), "\nMax: ", comma(Max, accuracy = ), "\nMean: ", comma(Mean, accuracy = .1))), color = "white") +
  labs(x="", y = "", color="", title = "Boxplots of MCA-Math score by employment state")+
  scale_y_continuous(labels=scales::comma,
                     limits = c(0, 6))+
  scale_fill_manual(values = brewer.pal(n = 6, "RdYlBu")) +
  theme_bar+
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        text = element_text(size = 18),
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 25, hjust = .9))

cte.achievement

Overview Cross-tabulation analysis examined the relationship between CTE achievement (CTE Concentrator or Completor, CTE Participant, No CTE) and workforce participation outcomes at 1, 5, and 10 years after high school graduation. CTE achievement shows a weak but statistically significant and strengthening association across all three time points, with a distinctly different pattern from the academic achievement variables examined previously.

Year 1 At one year post-graduation, CTE achievement shows a weak association with workforce participation outcomes (Cramer’s V=0.075). CTE Concentrators or Completors show the highest rate of meaningful regional employment (15.1%), compared to CTE Participants (11.4%) and No CTE students (8.7%). Postsecondary attendance rates are relatively similar across all three groups (54.0%, 59.2%, and 58.8% respectively), with No CTE students showing a notably higher rate of no Minnesota employment record (14.0% vs 9.6% and 8.8%). The ordering of groups by meaningful regional employment — with CTE engagement positively associated with regional workforce participation — is a substantively important early signal. (χ²=540.647, df=8, Cramer’s V=0.075, p<0.001)

Year 5 By five years post-graduation the association strengthens modestly (Cramer’s V=0.090). The divergence in meaningful regional employment across CTE groups becomes more pronounced, with CTE Concentrators or Completors showing the highest rate (28.2%), followed by CTE Participants (22.4%) and No CTE students (16.2%). Conversely, No CTE students show the highest rate of no Minnesota employment record (28.7% vs 21.7% and 22.2%) and the highest rate of postsecondary attendance (19.8%), suggesting that students without CTE engagement are both more likely to remain in postsecondary education longer and more likely to leave Minnesota entirely. (χ²=575.702, df=8, Cramer’s V=0.090, p<0.001)

Year 10 At ten years post-graduation the association remains weak but statistically significant (Cramer’s V=0.088). The pattern observed at year 5 strengthens further, with CTE Concentrators or Completors showing the highest rate of meaningful regional employment (29.1%), followed by CTE Participants (23.5%) and No CTE students (16.6%). Rates of meaningful Minnesota employment converge across all three groups (22.7%, 24.4%, and 24.3% respectively), while No CTE students show a strikingly high rate of no Minnesota employment record (43.8% vs 32.7% and 36.5%), suggesting substantially higher long-term out-of-state migration among students without CTE engagement. (χ²=310.756, df=6, Cramer’s V=0.088, p<0.001)

Summary CTE achievement shows a weak but statistically significant and consistent association with workforce participation outcomes across all three time points. Unlike the academic achievement variables examined previously, CTE achievement shows a pattern that is distinctly favorable for regional workforce participation — CTE Concentrators or Completors show the highest rates of meaningful regional employment at every time point, and this advantage grows over time. The clear ordering of groups by meaningful regional employment (Concentrator/Completor > Participant > No CTE) across all three years suggests a dose-response relationship between CTE engagement and regional workforce attachment. Conversely, students with no CTE engagement show substantially higher rates of no Minnesota employment record by year 10, suggesting that CTE participation is associated with stronger long-term retention in the regional labor market. This finding stands in notable contrast to the academic achievement variables, where higher achievement was consistently associated with employment outside the region.

  • One year after graduating
  • Five years after graduating
  • Ten years after graduating
data <- meaningful.emp.sw %>%
  select(grad.year.1, cte.achievement) %>%
  filter(grad.year.1 != "After 2023") %>%
  droplevels()

tab_xtab(var.row = data$grad.year.1, var.col = data$cte.achievement, 
         title = "", 
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/Claude/analysis_local_employment_ct/High school accomplishments/cte-achievement-1.html")

χ2=310.756 · df=6 · Cramer's V=0.088 · p=0.000


total.cte.courses.taken

Overview One-way ANOVA examined differences in total CTE courses taken across workforce participation outcome groups at 1, 5, and 10 years after high school graduation. Eta-squared values indicate a small but consistent association across all three time points, with the meaningful regional employment group consistently showing the highest number of CTE courses taken of any group.

Year 1 At one year post-graduation, total CTE courses taken differs significantly across outcome groups (F=388.03, df=4, p<0.001) with a small effect size (η²=0.032). The meaningful regional employment group shows significantly more CTE courses taken than all other groups. The attending postsecondary group shows significantly fewer CTE courses than meaningful regional employment (estimate=-1.674, p<0.001), as does the no Minnesota employment record group (estimate=-1.782, p<0.001) and not meaningful employment group (estimate=-0.901, p<0.001). No significant difference is found between meaningful regional and meaningful Minnesota employment groups, suggesting that CTE course-taking does not distinguish between these two stable employment outcomes at year 1.

Year 5 By five years post-graduation the association remains a small effect (F=240.62, df=4, p<0.001, η²=0.027). The meaningful regional employment group continues to show significantly more CTE courses taken than all other groups. The meaningful Minnesota employment group now shows significantly fewer CTE courses than meaningful regional employment (estimate=-0.975, p<0.001), a distinction that was not significant at year 1, suggesting that CTE course-taking increasingly differentiates regional from out-of-region employment over time. The no Minnesota employment record group shows significantly fewer CTE courses than meaningful regional employment (estimate=-1.316, p<0.001) and not meaningful employment (estimate=-0.513, p<0.001).

Year 10 At ten years post-graduation the association remains a small effect (F=187.13, df=3, p<0.001, η²=0.028). The meaningful regional employment group continues to show the highest number of CTE courses taken, significantly more than all other groups. The meaningful Minnesota employment and no Minnesota employment record groups are indistinguishable from each other (estimate=-0.012, p=0.998), while both show significantly fewer CTE courses than meaningful regional employment (estimates of -1.302 and -1.314 respectively, p<0.001). The not meaningful employment group shows significantly fewer CTE courses than meaningful regional employment (estimate=-0.783, p<0.001) but significantly more than both meaningful Minnesota employment (estimate=0.519, p<0.001) and no Minnesota employment record (estimate= -0.531, p<0.001).

Summary Total CTE courses taken shows a small but consistent association with workforce participation outcomes across all three time points (η²=0.032, 0.027, 0.028), notable for its stability rather than decline over time. Despite the modest effect size, the meaningful regional employment group shows the highest number of CTE courses taken at every time point, and the gap between regional and out-of-region employment groups grows over time — from no significant difference between regional and Minnesota employment at year 1 to a difference of approximately 1.3 courses by year 10. Together with the CTE achievement findings, these results provide consistent evidence that CTE participation is associated with stronger long-term regional workforce retention, even if the overall explanatory power of this variable is modest.

  • One year after graduating
  • Five years after graduating
  • Ten years after graduating
data <- meaningful.emp.sw %>%
  select(grad.year.1, total.cte.courses.taken) %>%
  filter(grad.year.1 != "After 2023") %>%
  drop_na() %>%
  group_by(grad.year.1) %>%
  mutate(q1 = quantile(total.cte.courses.taken, .25),
         q3 = quantile(total.cte.courses.taken, .75),
         iqr = IQR(total.cte.courses.taken),
         low.outlier = q1 - 1.5*iqr,
         high.outlier = q3 + 1.5*iqr) %>%
  filter(total.cte.courses.taken > low.outlier,
         total.cte.courses.taken < high.outlier) %>%
  ungroup()

labels <- data %>%
  group_by(grad.year.1) %>%
  summarize(Min = quantile(total.cte.courses.taken, .0),
            Q1 = quantile(total.cte.courses.taken, .25),
            Median = median(total.cte.courses.taken),
            Q3 = quantile(total.cte.courses.taken, .75),
            Max = quantile(total.cte.courses.taken, 1),
            Mean = mean(total.cte.courses.taken)) %>%
  ungroup()

plot <- ggplot(data, aes(grad.year.1, total.cte.courses.taken, fill = grad.year.1, group = grad.year.1)) +
  geom_boxplot_interactive() +
  geom_label(data = labels, aes(x = grad.year.1, y = 5, label = paste("Min: ", comma(Min, accuracy = .1), "\nQ1: ", comma(Q1, accuracy = .1), "\nMedian: ", comma(Median, accuracy = .1), "\nQ3: ", comma(Q3, accuracy = .1), "\nMax: ", comma(Max, accuracy = ), "\nMean: ", comma(Mean, accuracy = .1))), color = "white") +
  labs(x="", y = "", color="", title = "Boxplots of number of CTE courses by employment state")+
  scale_y_continuous(labels=scales::comma,
                     limits = c(0, 6))+
  scale_fill_manual(values = brewer.pal(n = 6, "RdYlBu")) +
  theme_bar+
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        text = element_text(size = 18),
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 25, hjust = .9))


girafe(ggobj = plot, width_svg = 10, height_svg = 10) %>%
  girafe_options(opts_selection(type = "none"),
                 opts_sizing(rescale = FALSE)) 
summary <- aov(total.cte.courses.taken ~ grad.year.1, data = data)

summary
Call:
   aov(formula = total.cte.courses.taken ~ grad.year.1, data = data)

Terms:
                grad.year.1 Residuals
Sum of Squares      18698.5  566411.3
Deg. of Freedom           4     47017

Residual standard error: 3.470871
Estimated effects may be unbalanced
anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)
  Tukey multiple comparisons of means
    95% family-wise confidence level

Fit: aov(formula = total.cte.courses.taken ~ grad.year.1, data = data)

tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/Claude/analysis_local_employment_ct/High school accomplishments/total-cte-courses-taken-1.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)
data <- meaningful.emp.sw %>%
  select(grad.year.5, total.cte.courses.taken) %>%
  filter(grad.year.5 != "After 2023") %>%
  drop_na() %>%
  group_by(grad.year.5) %>%
  mutate(q1 = quantile(total.cte.courses.taken, .25),
         q3 = quantile(total.cte.courses.taken, .75),
         iqr = IQR(total.cte.courses.taken),
         low.outlier = q1 - 1.5*iqr,
         high.outlier = q3 + 1.5*iqr) %>%
  filter(total.cte.courses.taken > low.outlier,
         total.cte.courses.taken < high.outlier) %>%
  ungroup()

labels <- data %>%
  group_by(grad.year.5) %>%
  summarize(Min = quantile(total.cte.courses.taken, .0),
            Q1 = quantile(total.cte.courses.taken, .25),
            Median = median(total.cte.courses.taken),
            Q3 = quantile(total.cte.courses.taken, .75),
            Max = quantile(total.cte.courses.taken, 1),
            Mean = mean(total.cte.courses.taken)) %>%
  ungroup()

plot <- ggplot(data, aes(grad.year.5, total.cte.courses.taken, fill = grad.year.5, group = grad.year.5)) +
  geom_boxplot_interactive() +
  geom_label(data = labels, aes(x = grad.year.5, y = 5, label = paste("Min: ", comma(Min, accuracy = .1), "\nQ1: ", comma(Q1, accuracy = .1), "\nMedian: ", comma(Median, accuracy = .1), "\nQ3: ", comma(Q3, accuracy = .1), "\nMax: ", comma(Max, accuracy = ), "\nMean: ", comma(Mean, accuracy = .1))), color = "white") +
  labs(x="", y = "", color="", title = "Boxplots of number of CTE courses by employment state")+
  scale_y_continuous(labels=scales::comma,
                     limits = c(0, 6))+
  scale_fill_manual(values = brewer.pal(n = 6, "RdYlBu")) +
  theme_bar+
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        text = element_text(size = 18),
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 25, hjust = .9))


girafe(ggobj = plot, width_svg = 10, height_svg = 10) %>%
  girafe_options(opts_selection(type = "none"),
                 opts_sizing(rescale = FALSE)) 
summary <- aov(total.cte.courses.taken ~ grad.year.5, data = data)

TukeyHSD(summary)
  Tukey multiple comparisons of means
    95% family-wise confidence level

data <- meaningful.emp.sw %>%
  select(grad.year.10, total.cte.courses.taken) %>%
  filter(grad.year.10 != "After 2023") %>%
  drop_na() %>%
  group_by(grad.year.10) %>%
  mutate(q1 = quantile(total.cte.courses.taken, .25),
         q3 = quantile(total.cte.courses.taken, .75),
         iqr = IQR(total.cte.courses.taken),
         low.outlier = q1 - 1.5*iqr,
         high.outlier = q3 + 1.5*iqr) %>%
  filter(total.cte.courses.taken > low.outlier,
         total.cte.courses.taken < high.outlier) %>%
  ungroup()

labels <- data %>%
  group_by(grad.year.10) %>%
  summarize(Min = quantile(total.cte.courses.taken, .0),
            Q1 = quantile(total.cte.courses.taken, .25),
            Median = median(total.cte.courses.taken),
            Q3 = quantile(total.cte.courses.taken, .75),
            Max = quantile(total.cte.courses.taken, 1),
            Mean = mean(total.cte.courses.taken)) %>%
  ungroup()

plot <- ggplot(data, aes(grad.year.10, total.cte.courses.taken, fill = grad.year.10, group = grad.year.10)) +
  geom_boxplot_interactive() +
  geom_label(data = labels, aes(x = grad.year.10, y = 5, label = paste("Min: ", comma(Min, accuracy = .1), "\nQ1: ", comma(Q1, accuracy = .1), "\nMedian: ", comma(Median, accuracy = .1), "\nQ3: ", comma(Q3, accuracy = .1), "\nMax: ", comma(Max, accuracy = ), "\nMean: ", comma(Mean, accuracy = .1))), color = "white") +
  labs(x="", y = "", color="", title = "Boxplots of number of CTE courses by employment state")+
  scale_y_continuous(labels=scales::comma,
                     limits = c(0, 6))+
  scale_fill_manual(values = brewer.pal(n = 6, "RdYlBu")) +
  theme_bar+
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        text = element_text(size = 18),
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 25, hjust = .9))

Post-secondary

We will analyze the following variables;

  • Post-secondary
    • attended.ps
    • ps.grad
    • ps.grad.InstitutionSector
    • highest.cred.level
    • ps.grad.location


Summary of Postsecondary Variables and Workforce Participation Outcomes

The following postsecondary variables were examined in relation to workforce participation outcomes at 1, 5, and 10 years after high school graduation. All variables are categorical and were examined using cross-tabulation and Cramer’s V. Variables are ranked by Cramer’s V at year 1, with the caveat that several variables show inflated associations at year 1 due to structural overlap with the attending postsecondary outcome category.

Categorical Variables - Ranked by Cramer’s V at Year 1

  1. Postsecondary Attendance (Cramer’s V: 0.698, 0.295, 0.108) The strongest association at year 1, largely driven by the near-tautological relationship between postsecondary attendance and the attending postsecondary outcome category. As this structural effect dissipates, the association weakens to weak by year 10. The most substantively important long-term finding is that non-attendees show stronger regional employment attachment while attendees show higher rates of meaningful Minnesota employment and lower rates of no Minnesota employment record.

  2. Postsecondary Graduation (Cramer’s V: 0.633, 0.330, 0.130) The second strongest association at year 1, also substantially driven by structural overlap with the attending postsecondary outcome category. By year 10 the association weakens to weak, with the most important finding being that postsecondary graduates show considerably higher rates of meaningful Minnesota employment while non-graduates show stronger regional attachment and higher rates of no Minnesota employment record.

  3. Highest Credential Level (Cramer’s V: 0.378, 0.246, 0.111) A strong association at year 1 that weakens to weak by year 10. A clear and persistent inverse relationship emerges between credential level and regional workforce attachment — sub-baccalaureate credential holders show the strongest regional employment rates across all time points, while bachelor and master degree holders show increasing tendencies toward meaningful Minnesota employment and out-of-state migration. Master degree or higher holders show the highest rate of no Minnesota employment record at year 10 (43.3%).

  4. Postsecondary Graduation Location (Cramer’s V: 0.158, 0.191, 0.162) A weak but remarkably stable and substantively compelling association across all three time points. Regional graduates show the strongest and most persistent attachment to meaningful regional employment at every time point, with rates consistently 10-15 percentage points higher than other graduation location groups by years 5 and 10. PS grad outside MN graduates show the highest rates of no Minnesota employment record, reaching 54.8% by year 10 — the highest rate observed for any subgroup across all variables examined.

  5. Postsecondary Graduation Institution Sector (Cramer’s V: 0.149, 0.161, 0.125) A weak but consistent association across all three time points. Public 2-year institution graduates show the strongest and most persistent regional employment attachment, with meaningful regional employment rates above 35% at both years 5 and 10. Private not-for-profit 4-year graduates show the strongest tendency toward meaningful Minnesota employment and out-of-state migration. These findings directly reinforce the graduation location results, as regional institutions are predominantly public 2-year institutions.

Overall Pattern The postsecondary variables collectively tell a coherent and substantively important story for regional workforce development. In the short term, postsecondary attendance and graduation status dominate the picture, largely reflecting structural relationships with the outcome categories. As these structural effects dissipate over time, a clear and consistent pattern emerges — the type, level, and location of postsecondary education shapes not whether individuals are meaningfully employed, but where they are employed. Sub-baccalaureate credentials, public 2-year institutions, and regional postsecondary graduation are all consistently associated with stronger regional workforce attachment, while higher credential levels, 4-year institutions, and out-of-state graduation are associated with employment outside the region or out of state entirely. The finding that graduating from a regional postsecondary institution is among the strongest predictors of long-term regional workforce retention has direct and actionable implications for regional higher education and workforce development policy.


attended.ps

Overview Cross-tabulation analysis examined the relationship between postsecondary attendance (No = did not attend, Yes = attended) and workforce participation outcomes at 1, 5, and 10 years after high school graduation. Postsecondary attendance shows the strongest association of any variable examined at year 1 (Cramer’s V=0.698), reflecting its near-definitional relationship with the attending postsecondary outcome category, and weakens considerably over time as postsecondary attendance becomes a historical rather than current status.

Year 1 At one year post-graduation, postsecondary attendance shows a very strong association with workforce participation outcomes (Cramer’s V=0.698). This is largely driven by the structural relationship between the variable and the outcome — virtually all individuals in the attending postsecondary outcome category attended postsecondary (26,953 of 26,954), making this association near-tautological at year 1. Among those who did not attend postsecondary, the dominant outcomes are not meaningful employment (39.7%) and no Minnesota employment record (31.6%), with meaningful regional employment accounting for 21.7%. Among those who did attend postsecondary, 75.8% are still attending at year 1, with the remaining 24.2% distributed across workforce participation outcomes. (χ²=23222.936, df=4, Cramer’s V=0.698, p<0.001)

Year 5 By five years post-graduation the association weakens substantially (Cramer’s V=0.295) as postsecondary attendance becomes a historical characteristic rather than a current status. Non-attendees show higher rates of meaningful regional employment (26.2% vs 22.8%) and notably higher rates of no Minnesota employment record (36.3% vs 19.6%), while postsecondary attendees show higher rates of meaningful Minnesota employment (19.8% vs 13.0%) and postsecondary attendance (22.1% vs 0.0%). The divergence in no Minnesota employment record between the two groups is particularly notable, suggesting non-attendees are considerably more likely to leave Minnesota entirely by year 5. (χ²=3064.364, df=4, Cramer’s V=0.295, p<0.001)

Year 10 At ten years post-graduation the association weakens considerably to a weak effect (Cramer’s V=0.108). Rates of meaningful regional employment converge almost entirely between non-attendees (25.2%) and attendees (24.7%). Postsecondary attendees show a notably higher rate of meaningful Minnesota employment (25.9% vs 15.6%), while non-attendees show a higher rate of no Minnesota employment record (40.3% vs 35.0%). The convergence in meaningful regional employment rates suggests that by year 10 postsecondary attendance history has little bearing on whether individuals are employed in the region, though it continues to distinguish between Minnesota and out-of-state employment outcomes. (χ²=234.106, df=3, Cramer’s V=0.108, p<0.001)

Summary Postsecondary attendance shows the strongest association o

  • One year after graduating
  • Five years after graduating
  • Ten years after graduating

  select(grad.year.1, attended.ps) %>%
  filter(grad.year.1 != "After 2023") %>%
  droplevels()

         title = "", 
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/Claude/analysis_local_employment_ct/Post-secondary/attended-ps-1.html")

χ2=234.106 · df=3 · Cramer's V=0.108 · p=0.000


ps.grad

Overview Cross-tabulation analysis examined the relationship between postsecondary graduation (No = did not graduate, Y = graduated) and workforce participation outcomes at 1, 5, and 10 years after high school graduation. Note that individuals currently attending postsecondary are excluded from this analysis as their graduation status is not yet determined. Postsecondary graduation shows a very strong association at year 1 that weakens considerably over time, with a pattern that closely mirrors postsecondary attendance but with important distinctions in the magnitude and direction of effects.

Year 1 At one year post-graduation, postsecondary graduation shows a very strong association with workforce participation outcomes (Cramer’s V=0.633). As with postsecondary attendance, this is substantially driven by the structural relationship between graduation status and the attending postsecondary outcome category — postsecondary graduates show an 83.7% rate of still attending postsecondary at year 1, reflecting those who have completed one credential and are pursuing another. Non-graduates show substantially higher rates of meaningful regional employment (19.3% vs 7.4%), not meaningful employment (31.2% vs 4.7%), and no Minnesota employment record (21.3% vs 1.7%), while graduates show markedly lower rates across all non-postsecondary outcomes at this early stage. (χ²=18207.912, df=4, Cramer’s V=0.633, p<0.001)

Year 5 By five years post-graduation the association weakens considerably but remains moderate (Cramer’s V=0.330). Non-graduates show higher rates of meaningful regional employment (25.7% vs 22.0%) and notably higher rates of no Minnesota employment record (30.7% vs 17.9%), while postsecondary graduates show higher rates of meaningful Minnesota employment (20.7% vs 15.1%) and postsecondary attendance (26.2% vs 4.5%). The divergence in no Minnesota employment record between the two groups is particularly striking — non-graduates are nearly twice as likely to have no Minnesota employment record as graduates, suggesting postsecondary graduation is strongly associated with remaining employed in Minnesota. (χ²=3791.997, df=4, Cramer’s V=0.330, p<0.001)

Year 10 At ten years post-graduation the association weakens to a weak effect (Cramer’s V=0.130). Rates of meaningful regional employment converge almost entirely between non-graduates (24.7%) and graduates (24.8%). Postsecondary graduates show a notably higher rate of meaningful Minnesota employment (27.9% vs 17.9%), while non-graduates show a higher rate of no Minnesota employment record (38.6% vs 34.3%) and not meaningful employment (18.7% vs 13.0%). The convergence in meaningful regional employment rates mirrors the pattern observed for postsecondary attendance, suggesting that by year 10 postsecondary graduation has little bearing on whether individuals are employed in the region but continues to distinguish between the quality and location of employment outcomes. (χ²=341.123, df=3, Cramer’s V=0.130, p<0.001)

Summary Postsecondary graduation shows a very strong association with workforce participation outcomes at year 1 (Cramer’s V=0.633), largely driven by structural overlap with the attending postsecondary outcome category, that weakens to a weak effect by year 10 (Cramer’s V=0.130). The most substantively important finding is the persistent and growing advantage of postsecondary graduates in achieving meaningful Minnesota employment across all time points, while non-graduates show stronger attachment to regional employment and considerably higher rates of no Minnesota employment record. By year 10 meaningful regional employment rates converge entirely between the two groups, suggesting that postsecondary graduation shapes where people work rather than whether they are meaningfully employed in the region.

  • One year after graduating
  • Five years after graduating
  • Ten years after graduating
data <- meaningful.emp.sw %>%
  select(grad.year.1, ps.grad) %>%
  filter(grad.year.1 != "After 2023") %>%
  filter(ps.grad != "Attending ps") %>%
  droplevels()

         title = "", 
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/Claude/analysis_local_employment_ct/Post-secondary/ps-grad-1.html")

ps.grad.InstitutionSector

Overview Cross-tabulation analysis examined the relationship between the sector of postsecondary institution from which individuals graduated (1=Public 4-year, 2=Private not-for-profit 4-year, 4=Public 2-year, 11=Private other) and workforce participation outcomes at 1, 5, and 10 years after high school graduation. This analysis is restricted to postsecondary graduates and those currently attending, excluding individuals who never attended or did not graduate. Institution sector shows a weak but consistent association across all three time points, with substantively meaningful differences in the type of employment achieved across sectors.

Year 1 At one year post-graduation, institution sector shows a weak association with workforce participation outcomes (Cramer’s V=0.149). The overwhelming majority of individuals across all sectors are still attending postsecondary at year 1, ranging from 70.5% for public 2-year graduates to 94.3% for public 4-year graduates, reflecting the expected pattern that 4-year institution graduates are more likely to be pursuing additional credentials. Among those in the workforce, public 2-year graduates show the highest rate of meaningful regional employment (15.3%), more than double the rate of public 4-year graduates (2.6%) and private not-for-profit 4-year graduates (0.9%), suggesting that 2-year institution graduates enter regional employment much more quickly. (χ²=2371.287, df=16, Cramer’s V=0.149, p<0.001)

Year 5 By five years post-graduation the association remains weak (Cramer’s V=0.161) but the patterns become considerably more interpretable as postsecondary attendance rates decline. Public 2-year graduates show by far the highest rate of meaningful regional employment (35.6%), more than double that of public 4-year graduates (16.2%) and private not-for-profit 4-year graduates (8.9%). Conversely, private not-for-profit 4-year graduates show the highest rate of meaningful Minnesota employment (28.5%), followed by public 4-year graduates (20.8%). Private other graduates show notably lower rates of no Minnesota employment record (8.3%) compared to all other sectors, while private not-for-profit 4-year graduates show the highest rate (24.2%), suggesting considerably higher out-of-state migration among graduates of private 4-year institutions. (χ²=2046.570, df=16, Cramer’s V=0.161, p<0.001)

Year 10 At ten years post-graduation the association remains weak (Cramer’s V=0.125). Public 2-year graduates continue to show the highest rate of meaningful regional employment (35.8%), more than three times that of private not-for-profit 4-year graduates (11.2%) and substantially higher than public 4-year graduates (21.0%) and private other graduates (24.6%). Public 4-year and private not-for-profit 4-year graduates show similar rates of meaningful Minnesota employment (30.7% and 33.7% respectively), while public 2-year graduates show the lowest rate (20.8%). Private not-for-profit 4-year graduates show the highest rate of no Minnesota employment record (40.3%), while public 2-year graduates show the lowest (28.7%). (χ²=519.468, df=9, Cramer’s V=0.125, p<0.001)

Summary Postsecondary graduation institution sector shows a weak but consistent association with workforce participation outcomes across all three time points (Cramer’s V=0.149, 0.161, 0.125). Despite the modest effect sizes, the findings are substantively compelling and tell a clear story — public 2-year institution graduates show the strongest and most persistent attachment to regional employment across all time points, with meaningful regional employment rates remaining above 35% at both years 5 and 10. In contrast, private not-for-profit 4-year graduates show the strongest tendency toward meaningful Minnesota employment and the highest rates of no Minnesota employment record, suggesting these graduates are the most geographically mobile and least likely to remain in the region. Public 4-year graduates occupy a middle position, showing moderate rates of both regional and Minnesota employment. These findings have direct implications for regional workforce development, suggesting that 2-year institution pathways are particularly important for regional talent retention.

  • One year after graduating
  • Five years after graduating
  • Ten years after graduating
data <- meaningful.emp.sw %>%
  select(grad.year.1, ps.grad.InstitutionSector) %>%
  filter(grad.year.1 != "After 2023") %>%
  filter(ps.grad.InstitutionSector != "Did not grad") %>%
  filter(ps.grad.InstitutionSector != "Never attended ps") %>%
  droplevels()

tab_xtab(var.row = data$grad.year.1, var.col = data$ps.grad.InstitutionSector, 
         title = "", 
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/Claude/analysis_local_employment_ct/Post-secondary/ps-grad-InstitutionSector-1.html")

highest.cred.level

Overview Cross-tabulation analysis examined the relationship between highest credential level attained (No college, Some college, Less than Associate Degree, Associate Degree, Attending ps, Bachelor Degree, Master Degree or higher) and workforce participation outcomes at 1, 5, and 10 years after high school graduation. Highest credential level shows a strong association at year 1 that weakens considerably over time, with substantively meaningful patterns in how credential level shapes the type and location of employment achieved.

Year 1 At one year post-graduation, highest credential level shows a strong association with workforce participation outcomes (Cramer’s V=0.378). At this early stage the results are heavily influenced by the structural relationship between credential level and the attending postsecondary outcome category, with postsecondary attendance rates increasing monotonically with credential level — from 0% for no college to 93.0% for master degree or higher. Among those in the workforce, a clear gradient emerges in meaningful regional employment, declining with higher credential levels — from 21.7% for no college to 1.9% for master degree or higher. Conversely, no college individuals show the highest rates of not meaningful employment (39.7%) and no Minnesota employment record (31.6%). (χ²=27300.114, df=24, Cramer’s V=0.378, p<0.001)

Year 5 By five years post-graduation the association weakens to a moderate effect (Cramer’s V=0.246) as credential attainment becomes more fully realized. A notable and substantively important pattern emerges — individuals with less than associate degree (35.1%) and associate degree (32.1%) show the highest rates of meaningful regional employment, considerably higher than bachelor degree (16.5%) and master degree or higher (8.1%) holders. The inverse is true for meaningful Minnesota employment, where bachelor degree (23.5%) and master degree or higher (11.8%) holders show lower regional but higher out-of-region employment rates. Master degree or higher individuals show the highest rate of still attending postsecondary (72.9%), reflecting ongoing graduate education. No college individuals show the highest rate of no Minnesota employment record (36.3%). (χ²=8561.286, df=24, Cramer’s V=0.246, p<0.001)

Year 10 At ten years post-graduation the association weakens to a weak effect (Cramer’s V=0.111). The patterns observed at year 5 persist and clarify further. Less than associate degree (34.4%) and associate degree (33.1%) holders continue to show the highest rates of meaningful regional employment, while bachelor degree (31.3%) and master degree or higher (29.1%) holders show the highest rates of meaningful Minnesota employment. Master degree or higher holders show the highest rate of no Minnesota employment record (43.3%), suggesting the highest credential level is associated with the greatest geographic mobility and lowest regional retention. No college and some college individuals show relatively similar distributions across outcome categories, suggesting that without any credential attainment the long-term workforce trajectory is similar regardless of whether some postsecondary was attempted. (χ²=746.232, df=15, Cramer’s V=0.111, p<0.001)

Summary Highest credential level shows a strong association with workforce participation outcomes at year 1 (Cramer’s V=0.378) that weakens to a weak effect by year 10 (Cramer’s V=0.111). The most substantively important finding is the clear and persistent inverse relationship between credential level and regional workforce attachment — sub-baccalaureate credential holders (less than associate and associate degree) show the strongest regional employment rates across all time points, while bachelor and master degree holders show increasing tendencies toward meaningful Minnesota employment and out-of-state migration. This pattern is consistent with and reinforces findings from the institution sector analysis, where public 2-year graduates showed the strongest regional attachment, and aligns with the broader pattern observed across academic achievement variables suggesting that higher educational attainment is associated with employment outside the region in Southwest Minnesota.

  • One year after graduating
  • Five years after graduating
  • Ten years after graduating
data <- meaningful.emp.sw %>%
  select(grad.year.1, highest.cred.level) %>%
  filter(grad.year.1 != "After 2023") %>%
  droplevels()

tab_xtab(var.row = data$grad.year.1, var.col = data$highest.cred.level, 
         title = "", 
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/Claude/analysis_local_employment_ct/Post-secondary/highest-cred-level-1.html")

100 %		 6068
6068
100 %		 1007
1007
100 %		 20060
20060
100 %
χ2=746.232 · df=15 · Cramer's V=0.111 · p=0.000


ps.grad.location

Overview Cross-tabulation analysis examined the relationship between the location of postsecondary graduation (Did not graduate ps, PS grad MN, PS grad outside MN, PS grad region) and workforce participation outcomes at 1, 5, and 10 years after high school graduation. This variable is uniquely relevant to the regional workforce development focus of this study, as it directly captures whether individuals pursued and completed postsecondary education within the Southwest Minnesota region, elsewhere in Minnesota, or outside the state. The association is weak but consistent across all three time points, with substantively compelling patterns in regional workforce attachment by graduation location.

Year 1 At one year post-graduation, graduation location shows a weak association with workforce participation outcomes (Cramer’s V=0.158). Among those in the workforce rather than attending postsecondary, regional graduates show the strongest attachment to meaningful regional employment (56.2%), substantially higher than PS grad MN (35.5%), PS grad outside MN (38.2%), and did not graduate ps (24.6%). Did not graduate ps individuals show the highest rates of not meaningful employment (40.0%) and no Minnesota employment record (27.3%), while regional graduates show the lowest rate of no Minnesota employment record (6.2%), suggesting that graduating from a regional institution is strongly associated with immediate regional workforce engagement. (χ²=1514.441, df=9, Cramer’s V=0.158, p<0.001)

Year 5 By five years post-graduation the association strengthens modestly (Cramer’s V=0.191). Regional graduates continue to show the highest rate of meaningful regional employment (42.4%), considerably higher than PS grad MN (23.9%), PS grad outside MN (23.9%), and did not graduate ps (26.9%). PS grad MN graduates show the highest rate of meaningful Minnesota employment (40.9%), while PS grad outside MN graduates show a strikingly high rate of no Minnesota employment record (47.4%), suggesting that graduating outside Minnesota is strongly associated with long-term out-of-state retention. Regional graduates show the lowest rate of no Minnesota employment record (13.4%), reinforcing the strong regional retention associated with regional graduation. (χ²=3181.250, df=9, Cramer’s V=0.191, p<0.001)

Year 10 At ten years post-graduation the association remains weak (Cramer’s V=0.162). Regional graduates continue to show the highest rate of meaningful regional employment (36.9%), significantly higher than PS grad MN (20.4%), PS grad outside MN (18.0%), and did not graduate ps (24.8%). PS grad MN graduates show the highest rate of meaningful Minnesota employment (38.0%), while PS grad outside MN graduates maintain the highest rate of no Minnesota employment record (54.8%) — the highest rate of out-of-state retention observed for any subgroup across all variables examined. Regional graduates show the lowest rate of no Minnesota employment record (23.2%), the lowest of any graduation location group across all time points. (χ²=1582.788, df=9, Cramer’s V=0.162, p<0.001)

Summary Postsecondary graduation location shows a weak but consistent and substantively compelling association with workforce participation outcomes across all three time points (Cramer’s V=0.158, 0.191, 0.162). Despite the modest effect sizes, the findings tell a clear and policy-relevant story. Regional graduates show the strongest and most persistent attachment to meaningful regional employment at every time point, with rates consistently 10-15 percentage points higher than other graduation location groups by years 5 and 10. Conversely, graduating outside Minnesota is associated with the highest rates of no Minnesota employment record at all time points, reaching 54.8% by year 10. PS grad MN graduates occupy a middle position, showing strong meaningful Minnesota employment but moderate regional attachment. These findings strongly suggest that the location of postsecondary graduation is one of the most direct predictors of long-term regional workforce retention, with regional institutions playing a critical role in keeping graduates employed in Southwest Minnesota.

  • One year after graduating
  • Five years after graduating
  • Ten years after graduating
data <- meaningful.emp.sw %>%
  select(grad.year.1, ps.grad.location) %>%
  filter(grad.year.1 != "After 2023",
         grad.year.1 != "Attending ps",
         ps.grad.location != "Attending ps") %>%
  droplevels()

tab_xtab(var.row = data$grad.year.1, var.col = data$ps.grad.location, 
         title = "", 
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/Claude/analysis_local_employment_ct/Post-secondary/ps-grad-location-1.html")
