Overall Summary of Variable Associations with Workforce Participation Outcomes

Cross-tabulation analysis with Cramer’s V and one-way ANOVA with eta-squared were used to examine associations between independent variables and workforce participation outcomes at 1, 5, and 10 years after high school graduation across four variable groups: demographics, high school characteristics, high school accomplishments, and postsecondary variables. Variables are presented in two separate ranked lists reflecting their respective effect size measures. An important methodological note: several postsecondary variables show inflated Cramer’s V values at year 1 due to structural overlap with the attending postsecondary outcome category, and should be interpreted with this in mind.

Categorical Variables - Ranked by Cramer’s V at Year 1

  1. PS Attendance (Cramer’s V: 0.698, 0.295, 0.108) The strongest association at year 1 but substantially inflated by structural overlap with the attending postsecondary outcome category. Association weakens considerably over time. Long-term finding: non-attendees show stronger regional attachment while attendees gravitate toward meaningful Minnesota employment.

  2. PS Graduation (Cramer’s V: 0.633, 0.330, 0.130) Similarly inflated at year 1 by structural overlap. Weakens considerably over time. Long-term finding: graduates show higher rates of meaningful Minnesota employment while non-graduates show stronger regional attachment and higher rates of no Minnesota employment record.

  3. ACT Participation (Cramer’s V: 0.430, 0.225, 0.137) The strongest non-structurally-inflated predictor and the most meaningful high school accomplishment variable. Non-ACT students show strong and persistent regional workforce attachment across all time points, while ACT takers are strongly associated with postsecondary attendance and subsequent out-of-region employment.

  4. Highest Credential Level (Cramer’s V: 0.378, 0.246, 0.111) A strong association at year 1 that weakens over time. A clear inverse relationship emerges between credential level and regional attachment — sub-baccalaureate credential holders show the strongest regional employment rates while bachelor and master degree holders increasingly gravitate toward out-of-region employment and out-of-state migration.

  5. PSEO Participation (Cramer’s V: 0.370, 0.165, 0.087) Strong at year 1, largely reflecting direct relationship with postsecondary attendance. PSEO participants show persistent tendency toward meaningful Minnesota employment over regional employment, suggesting broader geographic mobility associated with early college experience.

  6. Economic Status (Cramer’s V: 0.285, 0.127, 0.094) The strongest demographic predictor, particularly at year 1. Economically disadvantaged students enter the workforce earlier but into less stable employment, while non- disadvantaged students pursue postsecondary at higher rates. Differences narrow considerably by years 5 and 10.

  7. Special Education Status (Cramer’s V: 0.265, 0.149, 0.097) A moderate association at year 1 that weakens over time. Special education students enter the workforce earlier and show a persistent tendency toward regional over Minnesota employment across all time points.

  8. AP Exam Participation (Cramer’s V: 0.217, 0.146, 0.089) Moderate at year 1, weakening over time. Mirrors the ACT participation pattern — AP exam takers show persistent tendency toward out-of-region employment while non-takers show stronger regional attachment.

  9. English Learner Status (Cramer’s V: 0.214, 0.086, 0.059) Moderate at year 1, weakening considerably over time. English learners enter the workforce earlier but into less stable situations, with persistent challenges in achieving meaningful Minnesota employment through year 10.

  10. Non-English Home Language (Cramer’s V: 0.178, 0.058, 0.047) Closely mirrors English learner status throughout, consistent with the strong overlap between these variables identified during MCA. Slightly weaker associations at all time points.

  11. PS Grad Location (Cramer’s V: 0.158, 0.191, 0.162) A weak but remarkably stable association that is among the most substantively important findings of the entire analysis. Regional graduates show the strongest and most persistent regional employment attachment at every time point, while graduating outside Minnesota is associated with the highest rates of no Minnesota employment record, reaching 54.8% by year 10.

  12. PS Grad Institution Sector (Cramer’s V: 0.149, 0.161, 0.125) Weak but stable across all time points. Public 2-year institution graduates show the strongest regional attachment while private not-for-profit 4-year graduates show the highest geographic mobility. Directly reinforces the graduation location findings.

  13. Race/Ethnicity (Cramer’s V: 0.108, 0.044, 0.047) Weak and declining association. Notable patterns include persistent workforce challenges for AI students and higher rates of no Minnesota employment record among all minority groups at year 10. Small sample sizes for minority groups warrant cautious interpretation.

  14. Gender (Cramer’s V: 0.097, 0.093, 0.056) Consistently weak across all time points. Females more likely to pursue postsecondary immediately after graduation while males transition more quickly into regional employment, though differences narrow considerably by year 10.

  15. EDR (Cramer’s V: 0.079, 0.110, 0.124) Weak but uniquely strengthening over time. EDR 6E Southwest Central students show a growing advantage in meaningful Minnesota employment while EDR 8 Southwest students show increasing rates of no Minnesota employment record, suggesting growing geographic divergence within the region.

  16. CTE Achievement (Cramer’s V: 0.075, 0.090, 0.088) The weakest categorical predictor but with a substantively important and stable pattern. CTE Concentrators or Completors show the highest rates of meaningful regional employment at every time point, with No CTE students showing strikingly high rates of no Minnesota employment record by year 10 (43.8%).

  17. Community Demographic Description (Cramer’s V: 0.057, 0.072, 0.079) The weakest categorical predictor overall but also strengthening over time. Urban/town/rural mix students increasingly gravitate toward out-of-region employment while town/rural mix and entirely rural students show stronger regional attachment.

Continuous Variables - Ranked by Eta-Squared at Year 1

  1. MCA Math (η²: 0.168, 0.025, 0.017) The strongest continuous predictor at year 1 with a large effect size, the largest observed for any continuous variable. A distinctive early pattern shows lower Math scores associated with both not meaningful employment and no Minnesota employment record — unique among MCA subjects. By year 10 the meaningful regional employment group shows the lowest Math scores of all groups.

  2. MCA Reading (η²: 0.129, 0.023, 0.057) The second strongest continuous predictor at year 1 with a large effect size, notable for its unique trajectory — weakening to a small effect at year 5 before strengthening to a medium effect at year 10. The only continuous variable to strengthen at year 10, suggesting Reading achievement becomes increasingly predictive of out-of-region employment over time.

  3. ACT Composite Score (η²: 0.093, 0.038, 0.028) A moderate effect at year 1 weakening to small over time. The attending postsecondary group shows substantially higher scores than all workforce-engaged groups, and the meaningful regional employment group shows progressively lower scores relative to other groups over time.

  4. MCA Science (η²: 0.085, 0.033, 0.019) A large effect at year 1 weakening to small over time. Results should be interpreted with caution given the voluntary and self-selected nature of Science MCA participation. Pattern mirrors Reading and Math, with regional employment consistently showing the lowest Science scores.

  5. Total CTE Courses Taken (η²: 0.032, 0.027, 0.028) A small but remarkably stable effect across all three time points, unique among continuous variables. The meaningful regional employment group shows the highest number of CTE courses taken at every time point, with the gap between regional and out-of-region employment groups growing over time.

  6. Average Wages as Percentage of State Average (η²: 0.010, 0.004, 0.006) A negligible effect throughout, explaining at most 1% of variance. Despite very small effect sizes, regional employment consistently shows higher relative wages than meaningful Minnesota employment, suggesting competitive regional wages relative to the broader state labor market.

  7. Average Unemployment Rate (η²: 0.005, 0.015, 0.032) The weakest predictor at year 1 but growing to a small effect by year 10. Individuals with no Minnesota employment record consistently experience lower unemployment rates, likely reflecting out-of-state migration to stronger labor markets.

Key Themes Across All Variable Groups

Three overarching themes emerge from the analysis:

The first is a persistent academic achievement gradient in geographic mobility. Across virtually every academic achievement variable — ACT participation, AP exam, PSEO, MCA scores, ACT composite, and credential level — higher achievement is consistently associated with postsecondary attendance and subsequent employment outside the region. This pattern strengthens over time and is one of the most robust findings of the entire analysis.

The second is the importance of CTE and vocational pathways for regional retention. In direct contrast to the academic achievement pattern, CTE engagement variables — CTE achievement, total CTE courses taken, and by extension public 2-year institution graduation and sub-baccalaureate credentials — are consistently associated with stronger regional workforce attachment across all time points. This divergence between academic and CTE pathways has direct implications for regional workforce development policy.

The third is the critical role of postsecondary geography. Where individuals attend and graduate from postsecondary education is among the most stable and policy-relevant predictors of long-term regional workforce retention. Regional postsecondary graduation is associated with the strongest regional employment attachment while out-of-state graduation is associated with the highest rates of permanent out-of-state migration. This finding points directly to the strategic importance of regional postsecondary institutions for Southwest Minnesota workforce development.

master.after.ct <- meaningful.emp.sw 

write_csv(master.after.ct, "Data/SLEDS/Masters/After analysis/Master-after-ct.csv")
